entry.title: HU Close step in pack work center in RF with Outbound POSC in SAP EWM- Scenario
entry.content:
INTRODUCTION :

Process oriented Storage control is a feature which we extensively use in SAP EWM .It brings processes in consideration while building the process flow. In the configuration we have 'Auto  WT'  flag and we know that if we flag it ,then it creates the WT of the next process step automatically in the background queue method. But in reality what happens behind the scenes, we will try to see in this blog.

This blog will  give the basic understanding of the 'Auto WT' creation flag in POSC setting with HU  close option in RF (also in GUI in  general) and what happens if we miss to enable that. Will take step by step with scenarios what happens if this check is not flagged and when flagged.

The storage process ZPCK has 3 steps - Pick(OB01), Pack (ZOB2) and stage (ZOB3)

The Pack work center bin gets determined from the /SCWM/PACKSTDT - Determine Work Center in Goods Issue 

The storage process is assigned to WOCR and the  search sequence is with AA- CB01,

 

The example  delivery have 2 EA of pick quantity, where the source storage type is 0050, Pack work station is 8030 and the GI staging area is 9020.

Note :

The picking (OB01) step in this example is completed with a pick hu, which is confirmed to pack work center.

Will start the  scenarios with pack step (ZOB2)

Scenario 1: No Auto WT in ZOB2 step

Close HU step via RF, triggers the FM -/SCWM/RF_PACK_HU_CLOSE_PAI

The variable GT_MOVEHU is not updated if the ls_process_assignment-autfo field is not set, 

which is the Auto WT flag in the POSC step configuration.

This then goes in the BAdI /SCWM/EX_WRKC_UI_AFTER_SAVE  where it checks this field and if IT_MOVEHU is empty then it skips the entire logic to create the WT. Therefore, there won't be any automatic WT for  ZOB3  step.

Note in this case- user needs to manually trigger the WT via ad-hoc  moves

Scenario 2 : Auto WT checked in Z0B2 step


The ls_process_assignment-autfo  is not initial now. It then updates the GT_MOVEHU field.

The control then goes to the badi /SCWM/EX_WRKC_UI_AFTER_SAVE where it does check the POSC step and then triggers the WT in background queue with queue name with WMTH*

WT gets created automatically in background.

Important SAP NOTES:

3091718 - Close HU does not create the follow-up WT automatically

(https://me.sap.com/notes/3091718/E)

 

CONCLUSION :

This completes the blog. This blogs helps to understand the backend logic of HU Close and  'Auto WT' feature in POSC scenario. Sometimes missing a small check box makes it challenging to figure out. Hope it helps.

 

Thanks for your time. Please like and share your valuable comments.

Best Regards,

Y K B B Rudra Narayan

entry.title: Why don't named anchors work in Work Zone?
entry.content:
Why can't a drop a named anchor into a block of text in Work Zone? 

E.g.

<p>lots of text text text ... <a name ="section2">Section 2</a> text text text...</p>

I can write this into a text widget using the view source function, but if a link to this anchor is clicked elsewhere it doesn't jump the page to the anchor - it doesn't work like normal basic HTML works. 

This is annoying, there are lots of instances I'd like to link to content halfway down a page, as you can probably imagine for long knowledgebase articles. 

Please make named anchors work.

entry.title: Ensuring Inventory Accuracy End-to-End Configuration & Execution Guide (Physical Inventory)
entry.content:
Physical Inventory in SAP EWM is the structured process of comparing physical (actual) stock counts against book inventory (system-recorded quantities) to identify and correct discrepancies. This document covers the full configuration and execution of Physical Inventory in Warehouse PINT, demonstrated using product LAPTOPS (PINT COMPANY LAPTOP) as a real-world test scenario.

1. What Is Physical Inventory?

Physical Inventory is performed to verify that the system stock quantities match what is physically present in the warehouse. Discrepancies can arise due to picking errors, damage, theft, system posting errors, or unreported stock movements.

SAP EWM supports two counting approaches: Storage-Bin-Specific counting (count everything in a bin) and Product-Specific counting (count a specific product across bins). Both approaches follow the same document-driven workflow.

2. Physical Inventory Procedure Types

SAP EWM supports five Physical Inventory procedure types, each suited for different operational scenarios:

Annual Physical Inventory---AL---Full warehouse count executed once a year.
Cycle Counting---CC---Rolling count of subsets, reducing disruption.
Ad-hoc Physical Inventory---HL---On-demand count for specific bins or products.
Low Stock Check---LS---Triggered when stock falls below a threshold.
Put away PI---PI---Auto triggered during put away operations.

Focus of this document: Ad-hoc Physical Inventory (Storage-Bin-Specific) - the most commonly used approach for targeted, on-demand counts of specific bins when discrepancies are suspected, without requiring a full warehouse shutdown.

3. Physical Inventory Process Flow

The complete Physical Inventory process follows five sequential steps from document creation through final stock adjustment in S/4HANA:

Create PI Document ---- /SCWM/PI_CREATE ---Generate a Physical Inventory document for selected bins or products.
Count the Stock ---/SCWM/PI_PROCESS---- Enter the physically counted quantities against book inventory.
Post the Document ----/SCWM/PI_PROCESS--- Post the count — system calculates differences vs. book inventory.
Analyze Differences ---/SCWM/DIFF_ANALYZER ---Review and approve differences in the Difference Analyzer.
Clear & Update S/4HANA ---/SCWM/DIFF_ANALYZER ----Differences cleared and automatically posted back to S/4HANA.

4. Configuration
The following configurations must be completed in SAP EWM before Physical Inventory can be executed in Warehouse PINT.

1. Define Physical Inventory Area
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Physical Inventory Area → Define Physical Inventory Area
Physical Inventory Areas (PINT_0010 through PINT_0040) are defined to organize and group storage bins for counting. Each area is configured with behaviors such as Posting After Count, HU Complete, and HU Count. The overview shows all configured areas for Warehouse PINT:

 

 

 

Physical Inventory Areas overview - PINT_0010 to PINT_0040

The detailed overview shows the full list of Physical Inventory Areas configured across the system, including their complete settings for Put away PI, LSPI, LS Check, Tolerance Check, and HU Count:

Full Physical Inventory Area list showing all configured entries

For each PI Area, permitted document types (procedures) are assigned. PINT_0010 allows Annual, Cycle Counting, Ad-hoc, and Low Stock procedures:

Permitted Document Types for PINT_0010

2. Assign Physical Inventory Area to Activity Area
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Physical Inventory Area → Assign Physical Inventory Area to Activity Area
Each PI Area is assigned to its corresponding Activity Area to link the counting zones to the warehouse bin structure. Activity Areas 0010–0040 map to PI Areas PINT_0010–PINT_0040, respectively:

Assignment of PI Areas to Activity Areas

3. Define Number Ranges for Physical Inventory Document
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Define Number Range for Physical Inventory Document
Number Range 01 is configured for the year 2026 on Object /SCWM/PIDO, Subobject PINT, covering the full range of PI document numbers required for the warehouse year:

Number Range for PI Documents - Year 2026, Range 01

4. Specify Physical Inventory-Specific Settings in Warehouse
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Specify Physical Inventory-Specific Settings in Warehouse
Warehouse-level PI settings define currency (USD), fiscal year variant (K4), procedure ID (0001), and key behavioral flags. For Warehouse PINT, Owner and Party to Dispose are enabled, Corr. Perm. is checked (allowing corrections), and the NRI PI Document is set to 01:

Physical Inventory-Specific Settings for Warehouse PINT

5. Define Tolerance Groups
Three types of tolerance groups control how differences are handled at different stages and by different user roles. All three must be configured:

a) Tolerance Group for Recounting
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Define Tolerance Group for Recounting
Defines when a recount is required based on percentage or value. CLERK: 20% / USD 1,000. SUPERVISOR: 50% / USD 1,000. MANAGER: 100% / USD 10,000 (no recount required):

Tolerance Groups for Recounting - CLERK, MANAGER, SUPERVISOR

b) Tolerance Group for Posting Differences
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Define Tolerance Group for Posting Differences
Controls which differences can be directly posted without additional approval. CLERK: 10% / USD 100. SUPERVISOR: 50% / USD 1,000. MANAGER: 100% / USD 10,000 (unlimited):

Tolerance Groups for Posting Differences - CLERK, MANAGER, SUPERVISOR

c) Tolerance Group for Difference Analyzer
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Define Tolerance Group for Difference Analyzer
Defines value thresholds requiring review before stock adjustments post to S/4HANA. CLERK: USD 100. SUPERVISOR: USD 1,000. MANAGER: USD 1,000,000:

Tolerance Groups for Difference Analyzer - CLERK, MANAGER, SUPERVISOR

The three-tier tolerance framework (CLERK → SUPERVISOR → MANAGER) provides a robust approval chain. Small discrepancies are handled by clerks, while larger adjustments require supervisory or management approval — ensuring financial controls are maintained at every level.

6. Define Priorities for Physical Inventory

Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Define Priorities for Physical Inventory
Three priority levels are configured for Warehouse PINT: 1 = HIGH, 2 = MEDIUM, 3 = LOW. These help supervisors manage the counting workload and urgency:

Physical Inventory Priorities - HIGH, MEDIUM, LOW

7. Define Reason for Physical Inventory
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Define Reason for Physical Inventory
Six reasons explain why a physical inventory was initiated. Each is linked to a priority and marked Active: CCIV (Cycle Counting, Priority 2), LSPI (Low Stock Check, Priority 1), PTPI (Putaway PI, Priority 2), STND (Standard, Priority 2), UNAS (Not Assigned, Priority 3), UPLD (Unplanned, Priority 1):

Reasons for Physical Inventory - all six reasons for Warehouse PINT

8. Define Reason for Differences
Path: EWM → Physical Inventory → Define Reason for Differences
When a discrepancy is found, a reason must be assigned to explain it. Five reasons are configured: CUCL (Customer Claim), CORR (Correction), QIDF (Inspection Document), STOC (Stock Transfer), WTDF (Warehouse Task):

Reasons for Differences - five reason codes for Warehouse PINT


5. End-to-End Test: Physical Inventory for LAPTOPS

To validate the configuration, a complete Physical Inventory test is performed for product LAPTOPS (PINT COMPANY LAPTOP) in Storage Bin 0010-01-01, Storage Type 0010, Warehouse PINT. The test demonstrates the full count-to-posting workflow with a deliberate discrepancy.

Pre-Requisite: Verify Initial Stock
Before initiating the Physical Inventory, the Warehouse Management Monitor EWM and in S4  is checked to confirm the book inventory. Storage Bin 0010-01-01 shows 80 EA of LAPTOPS in Unrestricted-Use Warehouse stock (Stock Type F2). This is the book value the count will be compared against:

Warehouse Monitor - Storage Bin 0010-01-01 with 80 EA of LAPTOPS (book inventory)

Step 1: Create Physical Inventory Document

T.Code: /SCWM/PI_CREATE
Navigate to /SCWM/PI_CREATE and create a PI document for Warehouse PINT. Procedure: AL — Annual Physical Inventory (Storage-Bin-Specific). Storage Bin: 0010-01-01. Reason: UPLD (Standard Physical Inventory). Priority: 2 (MEDIUM):


PI Create - Procedure AL, Storage Bin 0010-01-01, Reason UPLD, Priority MEDIUM

The system confirms: "Document 10000 2026 created". PI Document 10000 is created with Status Active:

PI Document 10000 created - Status Active, Procedure HL, Ad-hoc Physical Inventory

Step 2: Process and Enter Count

T.Code: /SCWM/PI_PROCESS
Open PI Document 10000. Enter the physically counted quantity: 78 EA of LAPTOPS were found in the bin (book inventory says 80 EA). Count is entered by user MANAGER:

PI Process - Count Items tab showing 78 EA entered for LAPTOPS in bin 0010-01-01


After saving, the system confirms: "Document 10000 000001 counted." PI Status updates to Counted ;

PI Document after count - Status "Counted", 78 EA recorded

Step 3: Post the Physical Inventory Document
T.Code: /SCWM/PI_PROCESS
The document now shows Action = "Post Difference" with PI Status = Counted. The system has identified a discrepancy: 80 EA book vs. 78 EA counted = -2 EA difference. Click Post to commit the adjustment:

PI Document ready to post Action "Post Difference", Status "Counted."

The system confirms: "Document 10000 000001 posted." PI Status changes to Posted:

PI Document after posting - Status "Posted"

Final posted state - PI Document 10000 fully processed

Step 4: Review and Post via Difference Analyzer
T.Code: /SCWM/DIFF_ANALYZER
Once the PI document is posted, the difference is sent to the Difference Analyzer. Navigate to /SCWM/DIFF_ANALYZER for Warehouse PINT. Open the search dialog and check "PI Difference" to filter for physical inventory differences. The analyzer shows the -2 EA discrepancy for product LAPTOPS:

Difference Analyzer search dialog - PI Difference selected for Warehouse PIN
The detail view confirms the difference: Product LAPTOPS, Difference Qty = 2 EA, Diff. Value = USD 20.00, Priority 1 (HIGH), Posting Date 02/18/2026, Stock Type F2 Unrestricted-Use Warehouse:

Difference Analyzer detail LAPTOPS, 2 EA difference, USD 20.00, Priority HIGH

Select the difference record and click "Post" to apply the inventory adjustment. The system generates a Warehouse Task and confirms: "Warehouse task 23 created" - the stock correction movement is now physically executed:

After confirming the warehouse task.

Difference Analyzer Warehouse Task 23 was created after posting the -2 EA difference

6. Results and Final Verification
After the Difference Analyzer posting, the Warehouse Management Monitor confirms the stock in Storage Bin 0010-01-01 is now 78 EA of LAPTOPS (reduced from 80 EA), with the physical count weight reflected as 78 KG:

Warehouse Monitor Storage Bin 0010-01-01 now at 78 EA / 78 KG after PI adjustment.

The S/4HANA Stock Overview (T.Code: MMBE) confirms the inventory adjustment has been propagated from EWM back to S/4HANA. Total unrestricted stock for LAPTOPS shows 230 EA across the PINT COMPANY / PINT TECHNOLOGY plant. The AFS AVAIL FOR SALE storage location within PINT TECHNOLOGY specifically shows 78 EA, directly reflecting the Physical Inventory count result posted from EWM, confirming full end-to-end synchronisation:

S/4HANA MMBE (T.Code: MMBE) - LAPTOPS total 230 EA unrestricted across PINT COMPANY / PINT TECHNOLOGY plant; AFS AVAIL FOR SALE storage location reflects 78 EA, confirming EWM-to-S/4HANA inventory sync after Physical Inventory adjustment

Key Results Summary: PI Document 10000 created for Bin 0010-01-01 (LAPTOPS) in Warehouse PINT. Book: 80 EA | Count: 78 EA | Difference: -2 EA | Value: USD 20.00. Process: Created → Active → Counted → Posted → Difference Analyzer → Posted (WT 23 created). EWM stock adjusted to 78 EA. S/4HANA MMBE confirmed a total of 230 EA  full EWM-to-S4 integration validated.


7. Conclusion
This walkthrough demonstrated the complete Physical Inventory process in SAP EWM — from system configuration through to stock reconciliation in S/4HANA. The structured approach of Physical Inventory Areas, tolerance groups, priorities, and reason codes ensures that every count discrepancy is properly captured, reviewed, and resolved at the appropriate authorization level.

The three-tier tolerance framework (CLERK → SUPERVISOR → MANAGER) provides essential financial controls - ensuring that significant inventory adjustments always receive appropriate management approval before impacting the books.

For high-volume warehouses, configure Cycle Counting as the primary PI strategy rather than Annual Physical Inventory. By assigning cycle count classes (A/B/C) based on product value and movement frequency, you can continuously maintain inventory accuracy without the disruption of a full annual count, keeping the warehouse operational and the books accurate year-round.

entry.title: Archiving Product Using MM_MATNR in S4HANA Cloud Public Edition.
entry.content:
In this Blog we will go through the steps to archive the Product object MM_MATNR in the SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Public Edition.

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition there are functions exist to manage your data, one of them is information lifecycle management which is used to model and apply blocking and deletion rules and schedule archiving and destruction runs.

To manage the data lifecycle using ILM, you need to carry out the following:

1- Prepare your application to use ILM

This involves making all the required configurations for using ILM. It includes maintenance of audit area, assignment of ILM object to an audit area and maintenance of live policies and rules of residence and retention.

2- Schedule ILM processes

ILM Configuration:

1- Maintain Audit Area :

2- Assign ILM Object To Audit Area:

In this step we need to assign the ILM object to audit area for both RST and RTP policy category.

For RST - Residence Rule :

For RTP - Retention Rule:


3- Maintain ILM Policies and Rules :

For RST - Residence Rule :

 

 

 

For RTP - Retention Rule:

 

Schedule Archiving:

This process enables you to create a write variant and a preprocessing variant to be used for scheduling an archiving job.

Prerequisites

You must set the Mark for Deletion flag in the master data record. You can either set this flag for the whole record or for an individual organizational unit(s) of the record such as Sales, Plant, Storage Location, and so on.

Creating Variants

1. Open the Manage Archiving Variants app.

Note: Only archiving objects which are associated with ILM objects that are assigned to an Audit Area are displayed in the list.

Before ILM config:

After assign ILM object to Audit area in config:

2. Select the archiving object. The variant information is displayed on the right.

3. Choose Add New Variant.

4. On the Add New Variant screen, enter the name, description, variant type, test mode, action, and the selection parameters.

a- You can create a write variant or a preprocessing variant.

  • Select Write as the Variant Type to create a write variant.
  • Select Preprocessing as the Variant Type to create a preprocessing variant.

b- Choose the relevant Application Detail Log from the drop down menu.

c- Select the check box Test Mode to schedule the archiving job to run in test mode. If you do not select the check box, the archiving job is scheduled to run in production mode.

d- You can schedule an archiving or data destruction job.

  • Select Archiving as the Action to schedule an archiving job.
  • Select Data Destruction as the Action to schedule a data destruction job.

5. Choose Save.

Scheduling Archiving

1. Open the Manage Archiving Variants app.

2. Select the archiving object. The variant information is displayed on the right.

3. Choose the  All Variants tab.

4. Choose a variant.

5. Choose Schedule Archiving.

6. On the Schedule Archiving screen select the preprocessing variant if needed.

7. Choose Schedule. The archiving job is scheduled in the background. You can view the scheduled job by navigating to the Monitor Archiving Jobs app.

Important Note:

Scheduled archiving runs in the production mode are triggered by a technical job which is executed every two days at 20:00 UTC whereas scheduled archiving runs in the test mode are triggered by a technical job which is executed every two hours.

Monitor Archiving Jobs

With this app, you can monitor the status of the jobs for the archiving objects. For every archiving object that has jobs associated with it, the app displays the job statuses such as failed, scheduled, in process, and completed.

1. Open the Monitor Archiving Jobs app.

2. Select the archiving object.

3. Choose the Failed, Scheduled, In-Process, and Completed tabs to view the jobs.

 

4. You can click on a job to view its details along with relevant job log and application log information.

Finally, We get the following message while display the Product after Archiving the object :

Archive Explorer

With this app you can display archived data.

Prerequisites

You must have the business role template SAP_BR_EXTERNAL_AUDITOR (External Auditor) assigned to your user to be able to use the app.

Procedure:

1. Open the Archive Explorer app.

2. In the Archive Infostructure field, enter the name of the information structure on which you want to report.

3. Choose Evaluate to get to the archive infostructure selection screen.

4. Decide which data you want to read from the archive and enter the required values in the respective fields of the selection screen.

Note: If you don’t want to restrict the selection, simply leave the fields empty and carry on with the next step.

5. Choose Execute

6. Select an entry for which you would like to access the archived data and choose Display Object (Archive Access).

The system displays a table overview for the selected data object.

Conclusion: 

  • First, we prepare the ILM by maintain Audit Area, Assign ILM Object MM_MATNR to Audit Area and Maintain ILM Policies and Rules for both RTP and RST.
  • Second, create a write variant for this object.
  • Third, Schedule Archiving 
  • Fourth, Monitor the archiving jobs and its log details to make sure the process is successfully complete.

After those steps you can delete data at the end of its lifecycle and keep your system without undesired data.

entry.title: Solution Manager rises an MAXDB Log fill level error
entry.content:
Hello all, 

this might be a bit of a specific Error, however i wasn't able to find a true solution so i want to ask here. 

We get Alerts from our Solution manager at random times from two Content Server Databases with the following text:

Activated (Log fill level > 1.5 * Log segment size)

I am not really sure what that actually means and even more importantly, how to solve the issue. We do not seem to have any issues that the log runs full, or the System is not able to write down logs. So asking the AI, it states that the log Segment size would need to be increased. That however would mean i would need to recrate the DB as Log Segments cannot easily be recreated as Disk Segments. 

Has anyone encountered this?

Thank you very much!

Alessandro Büch

Please note that integration with third-party solutions such as Genesys depends on the specific integration scenario and is typically implemented via certified integrations or partner solutions.
For supported approaches and setup guidance, please refer to the SAP Integration and Certification Center (SAP ICC) and the SAP Help Portal, and verify whether a certified integration is available for your use case.

entry.title: Customer non valuated parts provided for production
entry.content:
We need to repair a machine for the customer. This machine article number is generally set up as a valuated material in our system, where each machine also gets a serial number. We would like to set up this process as kind of parts provided scenario where the customer send us the material and we perform some activities via production order. The material provided by customer should be non valuated in our system (no valuated stock, no invoice received). The customer in the end pays for the repair.


We would like to perform the repair via production order and not via the repair process in service - because the production team is also responsible for this process - there is no dedicated service team. We do not want to create a new non valuated article (machine) as we loose track of the serial number.

entry.title: How do you handle sensitive data when integrating external AI with SAP?
entry.content:
Hi everyone,

I’m currently exploring how to integrate external AI services into SAP workflows, particularly for use cases like document processing and support automation.

One challenge I’m trying to understand better is how to deal with sensitive data. In many scenarios, the data involved can include customer details or internal business information, and sending that directly to external AI services raises compliance concerns.

For those who have worked on similar integrations, how are you handling this in practice? Do you anonymise or preprocess the data before sending it out, or rely on other safeguards?

I’d really appreciate any insights or examples from real implementations.

entry.title: Different Table Partitionings in S4 HANA
entry.content:
Introduction:

Table partitioning in SAP S/4HANA is a fundamental database design method to enhance performance, scalability, and data management in a distributed environment. With a multi-host configuration, a large table is partitioned into smaller ones which can be stored in multiple index servers. This allows parallel processing, minimizes the time of accessing the data and evenly loads the system. The various partitioning strategies include hash, range, and round-robin which are based on data patterns and query needs. On the whole, partitioning assists organizations to work effectively with substantial quantities of transactional and analytical data.

Detailed Description:

Data is spread across multiple servers in a multiple-host SAP S/4 HANA system to enhance scalability and performance. HANA allows for various data distribution methods. Each index server is usually assigned to a separate host . Partitioned tables are divided into multiple partitions, each of which may be kept on a separate index server. The load can be balanced by allocating different tables to various servers. By enabling the same table to be present on several  servers, table replication speeds up joins and queries. The various partitioning methods include

Hash Partitioning
Hash partitioning is used to get around the 2 billion row limit and divide rows evenly among partitions for load balancing. Each distinct key value is mapped to one partition based on the hash result, ensuring even distribution of rows . The primary purposes of hash partitioning are to: properly distribute data among hosts in a multiple-node system. Boosts the execution of parallel queries by having each node scan its local partition. Because every partition manages distinct data slices, there is less competition for inserts and updates. Maintain manageable partition sizes while enabling large-table scalability.

Query for Hash partitioning,

Here, each CUSTOMER_ID is hashed internally by HANA, which then allocates the row to one of 8 th partitions.

CREATE COLUMN TABLE ZSALES_DATA (
SALES_ID BIGINT,
CUSTOMER_ID INT,
REGION NVARCHAR(20),
AMOUNT DECIMAL(15,2)
)
PARTITION BY HASH (CUSTOMER_ID) PARTITIONS 8;

Round-Robin Partitioning
In this case, rows are evenly and sequentially divided among partitions in a round-robin fashion. For example, first row in partition 1, second row in partition 2, third row in partition 3, fourth row in partition 1, and so forth. In situations where there is no natural partitioning key (no suitable column for hash or range), round-robin partitioning works best. When it comes to bulk loads or
analytics, you want parallelism and balanced data. There is no selective filtering; query patterns search through all tables. Because the rows are spread out across all partitions, it is inefficient for key-filtering queries.

CREATE COLUMN TABLE ZSALES_ROBIN (
SALES_ID BIGINT,
REGION NVARCHAR (20),
AMOUNT DECIMAL (15,2)
)PARTITION BY ROUNDROBIN PARTITIONS 4;

Range partitioning: it divides a large table into sub-tables (partitions) based on value ranges of a specific column, typically a date, numeric, or sequence field. Each partition stores rows that fall within a defined range. For example, data by year, month, or region code range .

CREATE COLUMN TABLE ZSALES_RANGE (
SALES_ID BIGINT,
FISCAL_YEAR INT,
REGION NVARCHAR(20),
AMOUNT DECIMAL(15,2)
)
PARTITION BY RANGE (FISCAL_YEAR) (
PARTITION P2019 VALUES LESS THAN (2020),
PARTITION P2020 VALUES LESS THAN (2021),
PARTITION P2021 VALUES LESS THAN (2022),
PARTITION P2022 VALUES LESS THAN (2023),
PARTITION PMAX VALUES LESS THAN (MAXVALUE)
);

Best practices in partitioning

Range partitioning is the default choice for time-series or housekeeping data, where most queries filter by a chronological or sequential column such as CALMONTH, DOC_DATE, or LOAD_TS. By using this method, the database can perform partitioning, which significantly improves performance by examining only the relevant date ranges when executing queries. Because entire partitions can be merged or deleted without affecting active ones, this also makes lifecycle operations, such as archiving or dropping old data, easier. However, a composite range-hash strategy is advised if recent time ranges accumulate disproportionate data (hot partitions). This
involves dividing by time range first, followed by hash sub partitioning for even load distribution.

Hash partitioning is used for key-based access patterns, such as when queries frequently filter on high-cardinality columns like CUSTOMER_ID or DOC_ID. It guarantees that data is distributed  evenly across nodes or partitions. This method helps scale workloads horizontally by supporting parallel processing during large joins and scans.

Rows are distributed evenly and sequentially in a circular pattern across partitions using round- robin partitioning, which ignores column values. Despite ensuring balance, it is independent of data, which means that it works best for staging, temporary, or transient tables that are used during bulk data loads or ETL procedures that frequently involve full scans. It should be avoided,
nevertheless, for selective queries or OLTP-style lookups because related rows might be located in different partitions, which would result in increased access overhead and worse performance for key-based reads

 

Summary:
SAP HANA partitioning techniques are each used in particular scenarios to maximize the database performance. The distribution of data is even and parallel queries can be executed using hash partitioning whereas range partitioning can be used with time-based data and is also more effective in managing the life cycle of the data. Round-robin partitioning is equally distributed in situations where there is no evident key but is inefficient when one wants to selectively query the data. The selection of appropriate partitioning strategy is based on the pattern of access and the nature of the data. Properly used, partitioning is a great way to increase scalability, speed of queries, and system efficiency.

entry.title: Re: Payment strategy Payment API
entry.content:
Hello!

 

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the “Pay” button in the Manage Payment Applications / Payment Requisitions app is not exposed as a public API.

 

The communication scenario SAP_COM_0727 (China Payment Requisition Integration) provides APIs for:

  • Submission of payment requisitions
  •  Approval and rejection actions
  •  Status tracking

However, it does not include:

  • Payment execution
  • Payment posting
  • Any API equivalent to the “Pay” button

 

The “Pay” action it triggers complex backend processes, including:

  • Payment strategy determination
  • Compliance and validation checks
  • Payment proposal creation
  • Financial posting
  • Bank file generation / integration

Because of complexity and regulatory considerations, SAP:

  • Executes these steps through internal logic
  • Does not expose them as public APIs in the Public Cloud

 

 Payment execution cannot be directly triggered externally, is handled internally via payment strategy and background processing.

 

Payment execution is system-driven, not API-triggered. Once a payment requisition is approved:

  1. It becomes eligible for payment
  2. The payment strategy determines how and when it will be paid
  3. The system processes payment

 

 

The recommended approach is to rely on automatic system-driven payment execution, not API-based triggering.

 

Optional approach for integration scenarios:

   - Submit Payment Requisition (via SAP_COM_0727)

   - Approval Workflow in SAP

   - Payment Strategy + Background Processing

   - Automatic Payment Execution

entry.title: Re: Different Table Partitionings in S4 HANA
entry.content:
Excellent technical breakdown, @Trupti81!

From my perspective in Industrial Systems Engineering, the importance of Partition Pruning in range partitioning cannot be overstated—it’s the difference between a query taking seconds or minutes when dealing with millions of logistics records.

I particularly liked your point about Round-Robin being ideal for staging tables during ETL processes. However, in a production S/4HANA environment, I’ve found that a Composite Range-Hash strategy is almost always the 'Gold Standard' for high-volume transactional tables, as it balances data lifecycle management (Range) with horizontal load balancing (Hash).

Quick question: In your experience, have you noticed a significant performance overhead when using Multilevel Partitioning for tables that are smaller than the 2-billion row limit, or do you always wait for volume to grow before implementing it?

entry.title: Re: Customer non valuated parts provided for production
entry.content:
Hi @Anja_1,

Since your production team handles these repairs and you need to preserve serial number history, I recommend using Special Stock Indicator 'C' (Customer Stock) instead of creating new material masters.

Technical Steps:

  1. Stock Receipt: Use Movement Type 501 C to receive the customer's machine into your warehouse. This keeps it non-valuated and specifically tied to that customer while maintaining the serial number record.

  2. Production Order: Add the machine as a component to your production order. In the component details, set the Special Stock to 'Customer Stock'.

  3. Financials: When you perform the 'Goods Issue' to the order, no valuation occurs for the machine itself. The order will only capture your labor and overhead costs.

  4. Billing: Use a Sales Order to trigger the production and eventually bill the customer for the repair activities.

From an Industrial Systems perspective, this 'Customer-Owned' stock approach is the most efficient way to keep your 'Clean Core' intact while providing full traceability of the machine's lifecycle.

entry.title: Re: Why don't named anchors work in Work Zone?
entry.content:
Hi @Robotperson,

You've hit on a classic challenge with SAP Build Work Zone and its Single Page Application (SPA) framework. The reason <a> name anchors fail is that the platform uses the URL 'hash' (the part after the #) for its own internal routing. When you click an anchor link, the system often tries to navigate to a new page instead of scrolling the current one.

A few things to try:

  • Use IDs instead of Names: HTML5 best practice is to use <h2 id="section2"> instead of the old <a name=""> tag. Work Zone is less likely to strip an id attribute from a header.

  • The 'Full URL' Trick: Sometimes, using a relative link like #section2 fails. Try using the absolute URL of the page followed by the #section2.

  • Widget Limitations: If the anchor and the link are in different widgets, the 'Shadow DOM' might be preventing them from seeing each other. Try placing both the link and the target within the same Large Text Widget to see if that resolves the jump.

From an Industrial Systems perspective, I agree—for complex documentation, these anchors are essential for navigation efficiency. It would be great to see the product team implement a standard 'Table of Contents' widget that handles this routing automatically!

entry.title: Re: Ensuring Inventory Accuracy End-to-End Configuration & Execution Guide (Physical Inventory)
entry.content:
Excellent end-to-end guide, @shivakiran!

From an Industrial Systems Engineering perspective, your emphasis on the Difference Analyzer as the final synchronization point between EWM and S/4HANA is spot on. It’s easy to forget that a PI count isn't truly 'finished' until that financial link is cleared in the back-office.

I particularly liked the inclusion of the Three-Tier Tolerance Groups. In large-scale maritime or industrial warehouses, this hierarchy is essential for maintaining SOX compliance and financial integrity during high-volume operations.

Quick question: In your Warehouse PINT setup, have you considered utilizing Low Stock Check (LS) during picking to further automate the PI process? It’s a great way to catch that -2 EA discrepancy the moment a bin is nearly empty, rather than waiting for an unplanned Ad-hoc count.

entry.title: Re: WT Creation Issue in S/4HANA Public Cloud EWM – Foreg...
entry.content:
Good day @_Harish_ 

Thank you for your question.

The behaviour you described points to incomplete automatic settings in your warehouse task determination, not an issue with the product master alone. Foreground WT creation can work because you can manually influence or confirm the bin. On the other hand background WT creation from Warehouse Monitor must determine everything automatically, so missing storage section search sequence, alternative bin type sequence, bin type, activity area, or WPT related settings can cause that failure you are experiencing.

I recommend as a next step to open the detailed application log object /SCWM/WME and sub-objects WHS_TASK, identify the exact missing determination step, and complete the corresponding config there. 

For the error in your screenshot, start with the checks from KBA 3640669, especially storage search sequence and alternative bin type sequence.

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: App or tool for massive upload condititons material i...
entry.content: Material listing/exclusion conditions cannot be uploaded using MASS because they are condition records. SAP provides Migration Cockpit object LISTEXCL_CND for mass upload. Alternatively, LSMW can be used.

entry.title: SAP CPI - Smart JMS Retry with Exception-Based Routing in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

SAP has released the Pipeline Concept for Cloud Integration as a robust solution for asynchronous message processing, bringing capabilities closer to what SAP PI/PO offered out of the box. It is an excellent resource — especially for complex ICO scenarios with multiple receivers, where it shines with receiver determination, interface splits, and sophisticated restart capabilities using Partner Directory and generic iFlows.

However, when your scenario has a single or small number of receivers, adopting the Pipeline Concept may become overengineering. In many Pipeline Concept setups, even a simple scenario may require multiple generic iFlows, JMS queues, DLQ handling, Partner Directory entries, receiver determination logic, and additional operational governance. For point-to-point scenarios, this can introduce more overhead than the business case requires.

For a simple point-to-point interface, this translates into a proliferation of artifacts, configurations in Partner Directory, XSLT mappings for receiver determination, and generic iFlows that add operational complexity without real business value.

For point-to-point and async request-reply where a sender communicates with a single receiver, JMS is the ideal choice when Integration Suite already provides the necessary mediation and orchestration.

Choosing the right integration model

Before jumping into any retry implementation, it is worth understanding the broader landscape of options available in SAP Integration Suite for asynchronous scenarios:

Pipeline Concept — the right choice when you have complex ICO scenarios with multiple receivers, interface splits, and recipient list patterns. It brings PI/PO-style pipeline processing to Cloud Integration with centralized governance through Partner Directory. The trade-off is significant operational overhead: XSLT mappings, generic iFlows, Partner Directory configuration, and strict naming conventions that must be defined correctly from day one. Correcting a poorly designed Partner Profile setup after go-live is a painful and risky operation.

Custom JMS per iFlow — the approach described in this blog. Simple, direct, and fully controlled per scenario. Each interface owns its queue, its retry logic, and its DLQ. The trade-off here is a different kind of proliferation: JMS queues are not exclusive to a single iFlow by design, but in practice each iFlow should own its own queue to avoid cross-contamination between message flows. In a large landscape this can lead to a high number of queues. SAP Integration Suite has limits on the total number of JMS queues per tenant, so this model requires serious governance on queue naming conventions and ownership from the very beginning.

HTTP Adapter Retry — useful for short-lived transient HTTP failures, but not equivalent to persisted asynchronous retry. It is adapter-level, limited in configuration, and does not provide the same persistence and DLQ governance as a JMS-based design.. Suitable for low-criticality synchronous scenarios where persistence is not required.

Data Store retry — an alternative when JMS quota is a concern. Messages are persisted in the tenant database and retried via scheduled iFlows. More flexible for manual reprocessing but adds database overhead and is not designed for high-throughput messaging.

When to use this approach

This custom JMS retry pattern is valid and recommended for scenarios with high criticality and a single or small number of receivers, where the overhead of the Pipeline Concept is not justified. However, it comes with clear prerequisites:

  • A strict naming convention for JMS queues must be defined before the first iFlow goes live. Example: <SENDER>_<RECEIVER>_<INTERFACE>_Q and <SENDER>_<RECEIVER>_<INTERFACE>_DLQ. Changing queue names after messages are in flight is disruptive.
  • Queue ownership must be documented — one iFlow, one queue. Sharing queues between iFlows creates ordering and redelivery issues that are very difficult to debug.
  • Tenant queue limits must be monitored. If your landscape grows, the number of queues can become a bottleneck and a migration to the Pipeline Concept may become necessary.

This blog presents a simpler, lightweight alternative — a custom JMS retry mechanism with one key differentiator: exception-based routing that decides whether a retry is actually worth attempting before wasting resources.

The Problem with Blind Retry

Most JMS retry implementations available in the community retry the message regardless of what caused the failure. This works fine for transient errors like network timeouts or server unavailability — but it wastes resources and delays DLQ routing when the error is non-retryable, such as a missing credential, a SQL syntax error, or an HTTP 401.

Custom retry mechanisms should handle transient errors such as network issues and HTTP 503, while avoiding retries for persistent errors such as mapping issues or authorization failures.

For large message payloads in particular, unnecessary retries consume JMS storage, worker node memory, and processing time — all for an error that will never resolve itself without a configuration fix.

The goal is not to replace the Pipeline Concept. The goal is to avoid using a pipeline-grade architecture when the scenario only requires controlled asynchronous retry for a point-to-point integration.

 

The Solution — iFlow Architecture

The solution uses three iFlows and two JMS queues: one processing queue and one DLQ queue.

IFlow 1 — Receives the message from the sender system and stores it in the JMS queue for asynchronous processing.

IFlow 2 — Reads from the JMS queue, executes the business logic, and in case of failure delegates to a Local Integration Process that evaluates the exception type and decides between retry or DLQ routing.

IFlow 3 — Reads from the DLQ queue using an exclusive JMS consumer with retries set to 0, attempts one controlled reprocessing step, and on failure triggers an alert via email, ServiceNow, or any monitoring tool. The flow should end with a normal End event to avoid creating another retry loop.

Iflow 1 representation:

Iflow 2 representation: 

Iflow 2 - Local Process representation:

Iflow 3 Representation:

SAP CPI Representation: 

The exception subprocess in iFlow 2 calls a Local Integration Process that first evaluates the exception type via a Groovy script, then routes to either an Error End event — keeping the message in the queue for JMS-native retry — or directly to the DLQ when the error is classified as non-retryable.

 

Routing decision for the retry:

Routing retry continue:

Key points:

  • SAPJMSRetries header must be added to Allowed Header(s) in Runtime Configuration and saved as a property in the first Groovy script at the beginning of the main flow.
  • SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed = false is mandatory for non-retryable errors — without it, the End normal event is ignored and the JMS adapter still retries.
  • The DLQ iFlow should have retries set to 0. Each company should implement their own alerting strategy — email, ServiceNow ticket, monitoring dashboard — since the DLQ message has already been classified as non-recoverable.

The Groovy — Exception Evaluator

The script below covers the most common exception types found in SAP Integration Suite scenarios. Each team should adapt the classification to their own environment — adding specific Oracle error codes, custom HTTP status patterns, BAPI return types, or internal application error signatures that are relevant to their landscape. The logic is intentionally straightforward: every exception either sets RetryExceptionValid = true and allows the JMS retry cycle to continue, or sets RetryExceptionValid = false combined with SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed = false to route the message directly to the DLQ without further retry attempts.

Covered out of the box:

  • JDBC — connection failures, transient locks, pool exhaustion, credential store errors, SQL syntax errors, Oracle ORA codes
  • HTTPS — connection refused, socket timeout, HTTP 4xx and 5xx status codes
  • SOAP — fault classification by env:Receiver (retryable) vs env:Sender (non-retryable)
  • OData — HTTP status-based classification, including 409 conflict
  • RFC / BAPI — communication exceptions, logon timeout vs auth failure, ABAP exceptions, BAPI RETURN TYPE=E and TYPE=W
  • Script / Groovy — runtime exceptions, missing methods or properties, class cast errors, and NullPointerException (see note below)
  • Mapping / XSLT / XPath — transformation failures, invalid XSLT, and XPath evaluation errors
  • XML / JSON Parsing — malformed payloads, unrecognised properties, and structural mismatches

A note on NullPointerException: in most CPI scenarios this reflects a logic defect in a Groovy script and is classified as non-retryable by default. However, if your scripts access optional external dependencies that may be temporarily unavailable, review this classification per scenario before deploying.

Default behaviour: any unrecognised exception is treated as retryable, assuming it may be transient. This is a safety net for unknown error patterns — not a permanent classification. As your landscape matures, review exceptions that consistently reach the DLQ without resolving and add them explicitly as non-retryable. Transformation, parsing, and script exceptions are always deterministic failures: a malformed payload or a broken XSLT will produce the same error on every retry attempt, so routing them directly to the DLQ reduces resource consumption and accelerates the feedback loop to the development team.

import com.sap.gateway.ip.core.customdev.util.Message

def Message processData(Message message) {

    def props     = message.getProperties()
    def exception = props.get("CamelExceptionCaught")?.toString() ?: ""

    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // JDBC
    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    if (exception.contains("JDBCConnectionException")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid", "true")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("JDBCTransientException")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid", "true")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("JDBCDataSourceException") && !exception.contains("CredentialStore")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid", "true")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("CredentialStoreCredentialNotFoundException")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
        message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("SQLSyntaxErrorException")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
        message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("JDBCCreatorException")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
        message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("ORA-00942") || exception.contains("ORA-00904")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
        message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
        return message
    }

    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // HTTPS
    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    if (exception.contains("ConnectException")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid", "true")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("SocketTimeoutException")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid", "true")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("Connection refused")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid", "true")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("HTTP 500") ||
        exception.contains("HTTP 502") ||
        exception.contains("HTTP 503") ||
        exception.contains("HTTP 504")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid", "true")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("HTTP 401") || exception.contains("HTTP 403")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
        message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("HTTP 400") ||
        exception.contains("HTTP 404") ||
        exception.contains("HTTP 422")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
        message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
        return message
    }

    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // SOAP
    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    if (exception.contains("SOAPFaultException")) {

        if (exception.contains("env:Receiver") || exception.contains(":Server")) {
            message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid", "true")
            return message
        }

        if (exception.contains("env:Sender") || exception.contains(":Client")) {
            message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
            message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
            return message
        }

        // Unknown SOAP fault — treat as retryable by default
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid", "true")
        return message
    }

    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // OData
    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    if (exception.contains("ODataException")) {

        if (exception.contains("500") || exception.contains("503")) {
            message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid", "true")
            return message
        }

        if (exception.contains("401") ||
            exception.contains("403") ||
            exception.contains("400") ||
            exception.contains("404") ||
            exception.contains("409")) {
            message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
            message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
            return message
        }

        // Unknown OData error — treat as retryable by default
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid", "true")
        return message
    }

    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // RFC / BAPI
    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    if (exception.contains("RfcCommunicationException")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid", "true")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("RfcLogonException")) {

        if (exception.contains("timeout") || exception.contains("Timeout")) {
            message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid", "true")
            return message
        }

        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
        message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("AbapException")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
        message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("BAPI") && exception.contains("TYPE=E")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
        message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("BAPI") && exception.contains("TYPE=W")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
        message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
        return message
    }

    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // Script / Groovy
    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    if (exception.contains("ScriptException")        ||
        exception.contains("GroovyRuntimeException") ||
        exception.contains("MissingMethodException") ||
        exception.contains("MissingPropertyException") ||
        exception.contains("ClassCastException")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
        message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
        return message
    }

    // NullPointerException: non-retryable by default in CPI context.
    // If your scripts access optional external dependencies that may be
    // temporarily unavailable, review this classification per scenario.
    if (exception.contains("NullPointerException")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
        message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
        return message
    }

    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // Mapping / XSLT / XPath
    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    if (exception.contains("MappingException") ||
        exception.contains("com.sap.it.api.mapping")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
        message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("TransformerException") ||
        exception.contains("XSLTException")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
        message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("XPathException")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
        message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
        return message
    }

    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // XML / JSON Parsing
    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    if (exception.contains("SAXParseException") ||
        exception.contains("SAXException")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
        message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
        return message
    }

    if (exception.contains("JsonParseException")          ||
        exception.contains("JsonMappingException")         ||
        exception.contains("UnrecognizedPropertyException")) {
        message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid",      "false")
        message.setProperty("SAP_MarkMessageAsFailed",  "false")
        return message
    }

    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
    // Default: retryable
    //
    // Any unrecognised exception is treated as retryable, assuming
    // it may be transient. This is a safety net — not a permanent
    // classification. As your landscape matures, review exceptions
    // that consistently reach the DLQ without resolving and add
    // them explicitly as non-retryable above.
    // ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

    message.setProperty("RetryExceptionValid", "true")
    return message
}

DLQ Strategy

The DLQ iFlow should be treated as a notification and manual review mechanism, not as another retry loop. Configure the JMS Sender of the DLQ queue with 0 retries. In the Exception Subprocess, send an alert email with the error details and end with a normal End event — this removes the message from the DLQ as completed, avoiding any infinite loop. Each team should implement the alert strategy that fits their operations model.

The DLQ consumer should also be validated against the selected JMS queue access type, since exclusive and non-exclusive queues can behave differently regarding retry, ordering, and error handling.

 

References

  • Introducing the new pipeline concept in Cloud Integration
  • Configure Asynchronous Messaging with Retry Using JMS Adapter
  • Retry failed messages in CPI with automatic and manual reprocessing
  • Handling Connectivity and Recoverable Errors in SAP CPI with JMS Queues
  • Configure Dead Letter Handling in JMS Adapter
  • JMS Mastery Series Part 3 — JMS in Production

Thank you.

Kind regards,

Viana.

entry.title: Re: Documentry batch
entry.content:
Hello @arturo_suarez 

SAP Help on Documentary Batches Prerequisites/Customizing

In general documentary batches are entered during goods movements, not on production or purchase orders - SAP Help Where Can You Enter Documentary Batches?

You can enter documentary batches in the following process steps:

  • Goods receipt for external procurement
  • Goods receipt for subcontracting
  • Goods issue for subcontracting
  • Goods receipt for order
  • Goods issue for order and network
  • Picking delivery to customer
  • Goods receipt for stock transport order
  • Picking outbound delivery for stock transport order
  • Goods receipt for return delivery
  • Goods issue for returns stock transport order
  • Goods receipt for return delivery for stock transport order

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Simplifying ABAP Proxy Migration: From SAP PO to Integration Suite (No MDR Required)
entry.content:
Introduction

This blog presents a complete walkthrough for migrating both outbound and inbound ABAP proxy configurations from SAP PO to SAP Integration Suite (Cloud Integration – CI). It is based on real project experience and focuses on the most direct and efficient path, leveraging SAP’s latest enhancements in ABAP proxy support within Integration Suite.

If you have explored other approaches involving MDR (Metadata Repository), SPXNMIG, or full redesign strategies, this method offers a clear alternative. When the objective is to transition existing proxy scenarios from PO to Integration Suite with minimal disruption, the approach outlined here is both streamlined and practical.

Why this approach — and how it differs from other approaches

There is already substantial guidance available on ABAP proxies, covering areas such as greenfield implementations, MDR-based migrations, OAuth configurations, and pipeline-based architectures in CI. Each of these serves specific use cases effectively.

This blog takes a focused, migration-centric perspective. It outlines a straightforward, end-to-end approach to moving existing proxy scenarios from SAP PO to Integration Suite using SAP’s current ABAP proxy generation capabilities. Both outbound and inbound configurations are addressed together, without introducing MDR or requiring a full pipeline setup. The emphasis is on simplicity, clarity, and direct applicability in migration projects.

Quick Comparison: What Changes, What Stays the Same

 This table shows exactly what changes and what the equivalent looks like after migration:

AspectSAP PO (Before)SAP Integration Suite (After)
Proxy Generation (SAP_PROXY_ESR)Points to SAP PO Enterprise Repository (ESR)Points to host from Process Integration Runtime service key (plan: API) via path prefix /rest/api/v1/workspace/sproxy
Message RoutingRFC destination > SAP PORFC destination > CI (XI Sender iFlow endpoint)
Proxy Visibility in SPROXYService Interfaces from ESR visibleService Interfaces from Cloud Integration packages visible
DependencyESR + SAP PO landscape requiredOnly SAP Integration Suite required
MDR Required?No (ESR is the source)No - new RFC (path prefix) update removes MDR need
Inbound EndpointPO routes to ABAP via internal engineCI > Cloud Connector > /sap/xi/engine in ABAP

Before You Begin — Prerequisites

Ensure the following are in place before starting:

  • Service Interface(s) already imported inside a Cloud Integration Design package (this is what SAP_PROXY_ESR will read from)
  • SAP Cloud Connector (SCC) configured and connected to your BTP subaccount
  • The user for the RFC destination must have the PI_Integration_Developer role collection assigned. For OAuth setup, a Process Integration Runtime service instance (plan: API) with AuthGroup_IntegrationDeveloper role is needed in BTP Cockpit.
  • S/4HANA or ECC system with sufficient basis access to configure SM59, SXMB_ADM, SMICM, SICF, and SPROXY

 

Part 1: Outbound Proxy Migration

Recalling how Outbound Proxy Worked in SAP PO

In SAP PO, outbound proxy communication is handled through two RFC destinations. Understanding both is important, as they map directly to their CI equivalents—only the targets change.

The first RFC destination, typically named SAP_PROXY_ESR, pointed to SAP PO's Enterprise Repository (ESR). Transaction SPROXY used this to browse available Service Interfaces and generate Proxy classes in the ABAP system.

Figure 1: SAP_PROXY_ESR RFC destination pointing to SAP PO's Enterprise Repository (SM59)

The second RFC destination will be configured in SXMB_ADM and handled the actual runtime message routing - every outbound proxy call from ECC/S4 was sent to SAP PO through this destination.

Figure 2: Mapping RFC destination for outbound message routing to SAP PO (SXMB_ADM – Config)

Figure 3: HTTP_PO1 RFC destination — routing messages from S4 to SAP PO (SM59)

Setting Up Outbound Proxy in SAP Integration Suite

What the new setup looks like

The concept is identical in CI — same two RFC destinations, same SXMB_ADM configuration. What changes is only where they point. Let's go through each one.

Figure 4: The outbound Service Interface in SAP PO ESR — this is what we are migrating to CI

Use SAP’s migration tooling (or a third-party alternative) to migrate the Integration Configuration Object (ICO) from PO to CI. After migration, configure the XI Sender adapter address in the iFlow—this becomes the target endpoint for the RFC destination in S/4.

 

Note: The Service Interface must be added manually to the iFlow after ICO migration (Add > Service Interface). It is not included automatically.

Step 1 - RFC Destination for Runtime Message Routing

Create an RFC destination of type G (HTTP connection) in SM59. This destination routes outbound proxy messages from S/4 to the XI Sender endpoint in CI. The path prefix must exactly match the address configured in the XI Sender adapter of the iFlow.

Figure 5: RFC destination for routing outbound messages from S4 to CI (SM59)

Figure 6: The iFlow in CI showing the XI Sender adapter address — this must match the RFC path prefix

 

Best Practice — Centralized Approach: While a direct 1:1 iFlow setup is useful for clarity, a pipeline-based pattern is generally recommended. A single XI Sender iFlow can receive all outbound proxy calls and route them internally using ProcessDirect to interface-specific iFlows. This enables a single RFC destination to serve multiple proxies, significantly reducing configuration overhead.

Step 2 - RFC Destination for Proxy Generation (SAP_PROXY_ESR) — The Key Change

With recent SAP updates, ABAP proxy generation can now be performed directly by connecting the backend system (ECC or S/4) to the SAP Integration Suite tenant.

Once configured, SPROXY reads Service Interfaces directly from CI design packages, enabled via the SAP_PROXY_ESR connection—eliminating the need for MDR or redesign.

Configure the RFC destination in SM59 with:

  • Connection Type: G (HTTP connection)
  • Target Host: Host from the Process Integration Runtime service key (plan type: API) — not the CI tenant host directly
  • Path Prefix: /rest/api/v1/workspace/sproxy
  • SSL: Active (Port 443)

Figure 7: SAP_PROXY_ESR RFC destination now pointing to SAP Integration Suite (SM59)

Step 3 - Authentication for SAP_PROXY_ESR

Before setting up the RFC destination, make sure your user has the PI_Integration_Developer role collection assigned in BTP>users. we have two ways to authenticate and for this blog we're using Basic Auth to keep it simple.

Basic Auth — straightforward, just put the S-User credentials in the Logon & Security tab in SM59. This is what we used in our project.

OAuth — you'll need to first go into BTP Cockpit, create a Process Integration Runtime service instance with plan API, and pick AuthGroup_IntegrationDeveloper during setup. That generates a service key which has everything you need — clientid, clientsecret and token URL. Detailed steps are covered in blog (referred at the end of this blog) on this topic, so I won't repeat it here.

Figure 8: Logon & Security tab — Basic Auth configured with PI_Integration_Developer role (SM59)

Figure 9: RFC destination connection settings — SSL active, port 443 (SM59)

Run the connection test. A response of HTTP 200 confirms the RFC destination is working correctly. Once this is in place, SPROXY will reflect the Service Interfaces available in CI packages.

Figure 10: Service Interface from CI is now visible and ready for proxy generation (SPROXY)

 

Note: Only the package with Service Interfaces within will appear in SPROXY.

Step 4 - Configuring Sender/Receiver IDs and IS_URL in SXMB_ADM

In SXMB_ADM, create the Sender and Receiver IDs that identify the participating systems. These IDs are then referenced in the IS_URL entry, which maps each outbound proxy to its target RFC destination.

Figure 11: SXMB_ADM — Creating Sender/Receiver IDs for the outbound proxy

Configure the IS_URL entry pointing to the RFC destination that routes to CI (e.g., XIMessage_toCI). The Sender/Receiver ID created above is entered as the sub-parameter.

Figure 12: Configure Destination (SXMB_ADM)

Figure 13: IS_URL entry pointing to the CI RFC destination

Once this is configured, trigger a test message from your ABAP program or test tool and confirm it flows correctly through to CI.

 

Tip - If your landscape still has both SAP PO and CI running during the migration phase, each outbound proxy will still need its own Sender/Receiver ID to enable selective routing — some proxies going to PO, others to CI. 

However, all proxies destined for CI can point to a single RFC destination — no need to create one per interface. This works best with a centralized pipeline iFlow, where one XI Sender iFlow receives all outbound proxy calls and routes them internally via ProcessDirect. Individual iFlows per interface would work too, but adds RFC destination or Sender/Receiver ID overhead as interface count grows.

 

Part 2: Inbound Proxy Migration

Inbound proxy scenarios operate in reverse—CI sends messages to the ABAP system, where the inbound proxy processes them. Since the ABAP system is on-premise, secure exposure is handled via SAP Cloud Connector.

Step 1 — Retrieve the ABAP System Hostname (SMICM)

In SMICM → Goto → Services, note the HTTPS hostname and port. This internal host will be mapped in Cloud Connector.

Figure 14: SMICM — Collecting the ABAP system hostname and port for Cloud Connector mapping

Step 2 — Configure Virtual Host Mapping in SAP Cloud Connector

In the Cloud Connector administration portal:

  • Define a Virtual Host (e.g., s4host.internal)
  • Map it to the internal host from SMICM
  • Use HTTPS protocol
  • Set system type as ABAP System

Add resource access:

  • / (full access), or
  • /sap/xi/engine (restricted access)

Then add the resource. For this blog's scenario, access to all paths is granted (resource: /), but for tighter security you can restrict it to /sap/xi/engine specifically.

Figure 15: SAP Cloud Connector — virtual host mapped to the ABAP system

Step 3 — Activate /sap/xi/engine in SICF

In transaction SICF, navigate to the path /sap/xi/engine and check whether the service is active. If it shows as inactive, right-click and activate it.

Figure 16: SICF — /sap/xi/engine confirmed active

 

Watch out - Multi-Client Systems: If your ABAP system has more than one client, the /sap/xi/engine service will default to calling the default client regardless of the URL parameter. To fix this: in SICF, open the /sap/xi/engine service > Logon Data tab > set the Client explicitly to your target client. This is a common gotcha that causes hard-to-diagnose errors.

Step 4 — Construct the Receiver XI Adapter URL in CI

The endpoint URL to be used in the CI Receiver XI Adapter should follows this pattern:

https://<VirtualHost>:<Port>/sap/xi/engine?type=receiver&sap-client=<CLIENT>

Use the virtual host name defined in Cloud Connector (not the internal hostname). This is the URL CI will call to deliver the message to the ABAP system.

Step 5 — Migrate the ICO and Update the Inbound iFlow

Figure 17: The inbound Service Interface in SAP PO ESR — this is what we are migrating to CI

Migrate the ICO from PO to CI using your preferred approach. After migration, open the iFlow and update the Receiver XI Adapter with the URL constructed above. The On-Premise connectivity should be set with the Cloud Connector Location ID.

Figure 18: Migrated iFlow — Receiver XI Adapter updated with the ABAP system URL via Cloud Connector

The Inbound Service Interface also needs to be added to the iFlow separately. It is not pulled in automatically as part of ICO migration.

Figure 19: Inbound Service Interface added separately to the iFlow after migration

Configure the delivery assurance and Quality of Service in the XI adapter based on your scenario requirements.

Step 6 — Activate the Service Interface in SPROXY

Open SPROXY in S/4HANA. The inbound Service Interface will now be visible via SAP_PROXY_ESR. Activate it to generate the corresponding ABAP proxy class.

Figure 20: SPROXY — Inbound Service Interface visible from CI, activated for proxy generation

Once activated, test the end-to-end flow: send a test message from CI and verify it is received and processed by the ABAP inbound proxy. Check SXMB_MONI in the ABAP system for message status.

 

Troubleshooting Guide

Below are the most common issues encountered during proxy migration, along with their root causes and fixes. This is based on real project experience and commonly reported issues in the community

SymptomLikely CauseFix
Connection test on SAP_PROXY_ESR returns HTTP 404using CI endpoint or wrong path prefix (path prefix is case sensitive)Verify the path prefix is correct. Also ensure the target host in the service key is the host from the Process Integration Runtime (plan: API) and not the CI tenant host directly.
Service Interface not showing up in SPROXYService Interface not inside a CI package, missed manual import of Service Interface after IFlow migrationCheck Service Interface is inside CI package
Outbound proxy message not reaching CIIS_URL entry in SXMB_ADM pointing to wrong destination or Sender/Receiver ID mismatchCross check the IS_URL mapping — namespace in Sender/Receiver ID must exactly match the proxy namespace
Inbound message from CI hitting wrong client/sap/xi/engine defaulting to system default clientIn SICF, set the explicit Logon Data client on /sap/xi/engine service

 

Summary

Migrating ABAP proxy scenarios from SAP PO to SAP Integration Suite does not have to be complicated. Here is the core of what this blog covers:

  • The SAP_PROXY_ESR RFC destination is the linchpin of this approach. By pointing it to Integration Suite instead of the ESR, SPROXY automatically reads Service Interfaces from CI packages — no MDR, no SPXNMIG, no inline type conversion needed.
  • For Outbound Proxy: update two RFC destinations (SAP_PROXY_ESR and the message routing destination in SXMB_ADM) to point to CI instead of PO. The rest of the SXMB_ADM Sender/Receiver ID and IS_URL config works the same way.
  • For Inbound Proxy: expose the ABAP system's /sap/xi/engine endpoint via Cloud Connector, configure the Receiver XI Adapter in your CI iFlow, and activate the Service Interface in SPROXY.
  • Watch out for: wrong client in SICF (multi-client systems), the target host for RFC: SAP_PROXY_ESR is from the Process Integration Runtime service key and not the CI tenant URL directly, and Service Interface not appearing in SPROXY (package not imported into CI).
Related Reading: For OAuth 2.0 setup with SAP_PROXY_ESR: "ABAP Proxy generation support with SAP Integration Suite" on SAP Community & ABAP Proxy Generation - General Procedure | SAP Help Portal on SAP Help Portal. For MDR-based migration: "Efficient ABAP Proxy Migration from SAP PO to Integration Suite" on SAP Community.

 

Thanks for reading. Questions or corrections — drop them in the comments, I'll do my best to help.

entry.title: Re: Foreign Exchange Adjustments for Cash G/L Accounts (M...
entry.content:
Hello!

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, the standard and automated process to calculate and post unrealized FX differences is Foreign Currency Valuation.

This process:

  • Revalues foreign currency balances at period-end
  • Calculates unrealized gains and losses automatically
  • Posts adjustment entries based on maintained exchange rates

Why it’s challenging this case based on current setup:

  • G/L accounts contain multiple currencies in the same account
  • Some balances are not a “true” reflection of FX exposure (due to clearing or mixed postings)
  • Profit center inconsistencies lead to unwanted or noisy postings

The limitation is not the FCV tool itself, but rather how the accounts and transactions are structured.

 

Possible options going forward:

Option 1 — Improve G/L structure

The most robust solution would be to gradually:

  • Separate accounts based on currency or business purpose
  • Ensure transactions reflect true FX exposure
  • Improve consistency in profit center postings

This would allow you to fully leverage FCV

Enables automation and reduces manual work

 

Option 2 — Use FCV Selectively

If a full redesign is not feasible:

  • Apply FCV only to clean and well-structured accounts
  • Exclude complex or mixed-use accounts

Provides partial automation and reduces overall manual effort

 

Option 3 — continue manual adjustments

The current approach using the Verify Currency Adjustment app:

  • Remains valid for complex or exceptional cases
  • Provides flexibility where automation cannot be applied

 

Option 4 — Hybrid Approach

A common and practical setup is:

  • Use Foreign Currency Valuation for standard, clean accounts
  • Use manual adjustments only where necessary

 

Option 5 — External Calculation + Upload

If more automation is needed without changing SAP setup:

  • Perform the FX calculation externally (e.g., Excel or another system)
  • Upload/post results into SAP using standard journal entry tools

 !!! The calculation logic remains outside SAP

 

Important to note:

  • SAP does not provide an alternative automated solution beyond Foreign Currency Valuation

entry.title: Feasibility Request – MRP Behavior for Reservations and PR Approval Status (Reorder Point Planning)
entry.content:
Dear SAP Support,

We would like to check the feasibility of certain client-specific requirements related to MRP behavior in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud (Reorder Point Planning).

1. Exclusion of Open Reservations from Available Stock Business Requirement:
During MRP execution, quantities in open reservations (where goods issue has not yet been posted) should not be considered as available stock.

Example:

  • Total stock: 100
  • Open reservations: 20
  • Expected available stock for MRP: 80
  • MRP should generate procurement proposal for 20

2. Exclusion of Unapproved Purchase Requisitions Business Requirement:
MRP should exclude purchase requisitions that are not yet approved and only consider approved PRs as incoming supply.

Request:

Kindly confirm:

  • Whether the above requirements can be achieved in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.
  • If any SSCUI, configuration, or standard functionality supports this.
  • Whether any in-app extensibility (e.g., BAdI) is available to control this behavior.
  • If not possible, please confirm whether this is a standard limitation.

We appreciate your guidance on this. 

Best regards
