entry.title: Re: Transportation Zone field needs to be add in conditio...
entry.content:
Good day @HO_IT 

Thank you for your question.

You cannot directly use KNA1-LZONE in SD pricing if it is not released in the standard sales pricing field catalog. Also, the fact that KNA1-LZONE appear inactive or commented in Eclipse should not be used as the basis for a solution in this environment. The Public Cloud supported route is not to activate or change standard technical fields. The supported route is to use the released extensibility framework and pricing configuration. See KBA 3569205 for details on the standard process for own pricing config.

In your case, this basically means the following:

  • Transportation Zone KNA1-LZONE cannot be forced into the pricing field catalog if it is not a released field.
  • You cannot use an Eclipse change to activate or expose this field for pricing.
  • You can create and use a sales order header custom field for pricing only if you created it in the correct sales document business context and published it for the usage you require.
  • You must enable the custom field for the relevant usage scenarios, otherwise it will not be available correctly in the pricing field catalog.
  • KBA 3475348 confirms that when a custom field is not added to the pricing field catalog and the system raises an error such as the field not existing in KOMG, KOMK, OR KOMP, the cause can be that the custom field was not enabled for the required UI, API, and SOAP API scenarios in the Custom Fields app.
  • If you enabled the custom field, it can be added through the released sales pricing field catalog process and then used in a custom condition table and access sequence.

I recommend the following:

  1. Use a sales order header custom field as the pricing-relevant field. You can create one, for example: YY1_TRANSPORT_ZONE_SDH, then populate it from the sold-to party or ship-to party Transportation Zone using released custom logic, depending on which partner should drive pricing. The pricing condition should then use the custom field, not direct KNA1-LZONE.  
  2. After you have created and published the custom field, add it to Change Field Catalog for Pricing in Sales. See KBA 3569205 for more detail on where to use it in.
  3. Do not use VK12 as the main design reference. To maintain condition record, use the relevant Manage Price-Sales app or the released pricing configuration process in your tenant.                                 

     

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: Customer non valuated parts provided for production
entry.content:
You are essentially describing a customer-provided material repair process in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, but with the constraint that the machine must remain tracked by serial number while not being valuated in your stock. Since the standard service repair process does not fit your organizational setup, here is how you can approach it

Possible approaches are

1. Use Non-Valuated Customer Stock with Serial Numbers
Configure a special stock indicator (customer stock) for the machine. Post the incoming machine to non-valuated special stock (no financial impact). Maintain the serial number assignment so you do not lose traceability. The production order can reference this special stock for repair activities.

2. Production Order with Customer-Provided Material
Create a repair production order where the machine is issued from customer stock (non-valuated). The order consumes spare parts (valuated) and activities (labor). Settlement posts costs to a repair service billing document, not to the machine itself.

3. Billing the Repair Service
Customer pays for the repair service (labor + parts). The machine itself is returned without valuation impact. Use a service material (non-stock, non-valuated) for billing purposes, linked to the production order.

entry.title: Re: MR11 change layout (grid) is not working
entry.content:
Hi @bill94 ,

My friend if your MR11 layout is breaking because the ALV layout configuration is getting corrupted—usually due to an incompatible field, a saved variant issue, or an ALV cache problem. Resetting or deleting the affected layout/variant typically fixes it.

To fix this issue you can do following as mentioned below.

1. Reset the layout to SAP standard, This is the fastest fix.
Go to MR11
Open Change Layout
Choose Layout → Manage
Delete your custom layout or reset to SAP standard
Rebuild the layout from scratch
This works because the corrupted variant is no longer loaded.

2. Delete your user-specific ALV variant, If the issue is tied to your user:
Go to Settings → Layout → Manage
Look for variants with your user name
Delete them
Log out and back in
Try again

You can try MR11SHOW, this uses a similar ALV structure but sometimes handles layouts more gracefully. If MR11SHOW works normally, it confirms the issue is with the MR11 layout variant, not the data.

Hope your issue got fixed, if so then accept my solution.

entry.title: Orchestrating Autonomous Coding Agents - Manage Your Personal AI Dev Team!
entry.content:
Today, AI-assisted coding may look something like this: a developer pulls a ticket from a Jira backlog, generates a prompt for their coding agent, iterates on the AI-generated code with the agent, and finally opens a PR to close the ticket in a review process with the team.
 
This approach significantly speeds up implementation, allows for shorter iteration cycles, and offloads straightforward, tedious tasks to automation. However, it still relies on a human to proactively kick off and babysit a single agent. This is why a new paradigm has recently emerged. Instead of assigning tickets to humans, some tickets are directly assigned to autonomous coding agents, which then spawn instances that draft PRs on separate branches in parallel. Humans code less and less, iterate using prompts, and focus on review tasks rather than coding.
 
This article discusses the benefits and pitfalls of orchestrating autonomous coding agents through issues and tickets, sheds light on how this approach can be integrated into the team and workflow of a software project, references successful industry examples, and points you to frameworks that help you get started with the orchestration of your very own agent dev team. Finally, we share a specific setup you can easily replicate to enable autonomous Claude Code agents in your git repository, leveraging SAP AI Core underneath.


Server-Side Autonomous Coding Agents – Idea and Bigger Picture

The fundamental idea of server-side autonomous coding agents is to move from having a dedicated tool on your local PC to interacting with remote agent coworkers. Instead of operating in a micro-managed, pair-programming-like setting, autonomous coding agents work independently for longer periods. They make decisions as they go and come back with a pull request when they are done: just like a human colleague!

This could enable human contributors to scale their work considerably, moving from individual agent sessions to parallel agent sessions and thus enabling faster iteration cycles. At this point, it's not about using coding agents only; it's about scaling a human dev team by integrating multiple, autonomous agent workers and controlling that scaling without compromising code quality or security.

This leaves human developers in a management position: they focus on supervisory work such as task definition, code review, and test-driven verification. It is only natural to leverage existing, well-established platforms and processes to fulfill these oversight duties:



Reference Architecture: Server-Side, Autonomous Coding Agents Produce Pull Requests Under Human Guidance

 

Orchestrating server-side autonomous coding agents through typical human communication channels like chat or email, handing off tasks via project management solutions like Jira or GitHub Projects, and reviewing finished work in developer platforms via pull requests offers the added advantage of working with both human and AI colleagues. 

This all sounds great, but what's the catch?

The ability of AI to complete long coding tasks is high and growing, even for tasks that would take a human several hours.  At the same time, agents are potentially faster and more cost-efficient than humans. This makes assigning whole tickets to them both feasible and attractive for cutting costs and increasing development speed. However, coding agents still make mistakes and can produce low-quality code. Andrej Karpathy recently summarized prototypical shortcomings of coding agents. He points out that they are prone to:

  • producing inefficient or sloppy architectures and concepts
  • bloating and over-complicating code
  • ignoring code maintainability
  • leaving outdated and unused code hanging after completing primary implementation goals
  • going beyond their task, working on code within the blast radius  

While these issues may be solved eventually, we are not yet living in a world where humans exclusively focus on product management, goal setting, and design. Instead, it is paramount that developers and architects review agent-generated PRs thoroughly and iterate on code by requesting changes or taking over crunch-time aspects of a ticket. This also means that development speed is currently capped by the team's review bandwidth rather than their implementation velocity. 

While we cannot pipe feature requests into a fully autonomous agent system that cuts the product requirements document into tickets, implements them, delivers pull requests, and ultimately returns with a new product release, development teams are certainly in a position to start thinking in that direction. Consider the following ways in which software development teams can establish an AI-ready development process that leverages the strengths of autonomous coding agents while mitigating their current flaws:

 

  • Make Supporting Documents Accessible to Coding Agents: Supporting documents like product requirements documents (PRD), architecture decision records (ADR), and documentation help agents understand the context of what you are building beyond what's clear from the code base and task. Specifically, PRD and ADR help autonomous coding agents see not only what has been built but also what shall be built from hereon. Dev teams can grant them access by either maintaining supporting documents within the same codebase (e.g., as Markdown files) or by providing indirect access through tools (e.g., an MCP connection to Aha!, Jira, Document Grounding, ...).
  • Embrace Implementation Speed, Govern Essentials, Take Responsibility, and Trace Usage: Looking at the shortcomings of today's coding agents, human developers must watch out for a clean architecture and code maintainability. The industry standard is to use coding agents in tightly controlled settings. Recently, the Linux Kernel Development Community has published its rules regarding the use of AI coding agents. While allowing the use of coding agents, they clarify that all contributors must take full responsibility for their code, review licensing requirements, sign off on commits with their accounts, and document the specific setup used to support implementation. Documentation includes agent names, model version, and significant tools. FOSS communities like matplotlib tend to allow generative AI for tasks that the responsible contributor can also solve themselves. Both examples stress the importance of human responsibility and source traceability. How that translates to autonomous coding agents is an open question. A good first approach might be to assign a responsible human who must finally sign off on the agent's code. Still, we are aware that this effectively bottlenecks the potential of autonomous coding agents.
  • Consider Agent Resources and Capabilities in Sprint Planning: With coding agents in the mix, agile development teams should be aware of project-specific learnings regarding agent reliability and use cases and consider them in their sprint planning. Building on past experiences, they can scope tickets that maximize autonomy and minimize the expected number of iterations on agent tickets. They might also choose to consciously hand off crucial tickets to humans, i.e., those that define architectures or have proven to be problematic in agentic settings in the past.
  • Support Junior Developers in Developing a Critical Mindset: With human developers mostly taking on the hardest and most decisive tasks of a software project and agents doing the ground work, critical thinking and system understanding become more important than ever. This raises an important question: how do junior developers skill up and learn to be critical of agent-generated code when coding agents easily solve entry-level tasks? We do not have a definitive answer to that. Still, it might be at least threefold: first, learning issues could be left intentionally open to provide ramp-up opportunities for new colleagues. This practice is currently strongly defended by, e.g., the matplotlib community. New colleagues would then work on these issues manually or in close collaboration with an agent. Second, pair programming, mentoring, and educational materials may help starters develop the necessary skills and critical mindset. Third, as the reliability of coding agents improves, many developers may use evals to validate implementations indirectly rather than checking code, effectively raising the level of abstraction. At this point, AI coding frameworks serve as operating systems or compilers. Most developers do not audit them regularly; instead, they develop on top of them.

 

These considerations may help anticipate and work with the principal strengths and weaknesses of autonomous coding agents in software projects, but how do coding agents fare in practice? How much more code per time can you expect to produce today? What are the implications on code quality, and – should you decide to try for yourself – how can you set up your own team of autonomous coding agents?

 

Industry Examples, Research, and Frameworks

 

Recently, autonomous coding agents have gained momentum in the industry. Famous case studies were published by Stripe and Spotify, reporting that more than 1000 pull requests were merged each week, with code generated entirely by coding agents. In these reports, humans only supervised through reviews. Both setups share some similarities, such as automatic feedback loops based on linting and testing, while also having some differences, such as the underlying coding agent: Spotify relies mostly on Claude Code, while Stripe uses a custom agent harness.

Academic research has also taken up the topic. Agarwal et al. studied the introduction of autonomous coding agents into two kinds of repository types: (1) Repositories with no prior coding agent involvement and (2) Repositories where IDE coding agents were already used before. For (1), velocity gains were discovered (+36.3% commits and +76.6% lines). However, for (2), essentially no gain was present (+3.1% commits and -6.3% lines) after adding an autonomous coding agent. In addition, for both types, quality degradation, front-loaded velocity gains, and decreasing code quality were reported. Based on the findings, the authors highlight the need for selective deployment and active oversight. Suggestions for future work revolve around the study of long-horizon post-adoption usage and collaboration patterns that balance acceleration with code quality. Overall, we see mixed reactions to autonomous coding agents. Reports evolve between enthusiasm and skepticism as they report favorable, neutral, and critical numbers. 

We think autonomous coding agents will not go away, as they have shown significant potential for practical applications. We are sure that their role and autonomy in software projects will increase over time. However, research and experience clearly show that we still have much to learn about this technology and its integration into development cycles. Adapting autonomous coding agents will be a process, and adaptation speeds will differ from product to product. Until the dust settles and we have stable, industry-wide learnings, many actors should try out autonomous coding agents for themselves. After all, this is not a trend to overlook!

To get you started, you may want to check out the following tools and frameworks that enable you to collect hands-on experience in your projects:


Tools and Frameworks to Set Up and Orchestrate Autonomous Coding Agents

 Basic setupEntry pointKey strengthsMain trade-offs
Claude Code GitHub ActionCustom GitHub Action + Claude CodeEvery possible GitHub workflow trigger (mainly issue/PR creation + comments)GitHub-native automation, powerful Claude Code coding agent, full Claude Code configuration possibilitiesLimited to Claude Code coding agent, requires careful workflow setup and permission scoping, no full issue-tracker and orchestration layer
GitHub Copilot Cloud AgentsGitHub native (Action) + GitHub Copilot Cloud Agent Issues, Agents Panel, Copilot Chat, GitHub CLI, IDEs, MCP, Jira, Slack, Teams, Linear, ...Many different integrations and entry points, Support for multiple top-tier models (Google, OpenAI, Anthropic)Less customizable than self-built workflows and integrations, no self-hosted option since the Agent is running in the cloud
n8nWorkflow automation + coding agent integrationsEverything that n8n supports natively or via community nodesExtensive number of integrations, visual workflow builderNo native coding agent solution (only community integrations of coding agents, such as Claude Code into n8n)
OpenClawAutonomous agent + coding agent integrationsEverything that OpenClaw supports natively or via community pluginsExtensive number of integrations, high level of autonomyNo native coding agent solution, Limited guardrails and potential security problems (NemoClaw could solve some)



How to Set Up Your Coding Agent Team Using a Claude Code Action and SAP AI Core

 

In this section, we walk through how we added an autonomous coding agent to our GitHub repositories using a Claude Code Action and SAP AI Core. You can easily replicate this setup to get started with autonomous coding agents on top of SAP AI Core.

What is Claude Code Action?

A Claude Code Action is a GitHub Action that lets Claude work directly in GitHub. It can do so in two different modes:
  • In tag mode, a human initiates the process by writing something like `@claude fix the login bug` in an issue or PR comment. Claude reads the conversation context and acts on the request. This is the conversational, human-initiated path, similar to how you would ask a colleague for help.
  • In agent mode, no human needs to call the agent explicitly. Instead, any GitHub event can trigger the Claude Code Action. This may be a PR being opened, a cron schedule, a label being applied, or a CI failure. The event then supplies context to the action. This is what enables fully automated workflows, such as auto-reviewing every PR, triaging issues on creation, or running weekly repository maintenance.
What makes Claude Code Action so relevant to orchestrating autonomous coding agents is that it addresses many of the integration requirements outlined earlier. It hooks into GitHub's event system (like issue creation, comments, assignments), runs in a headless server session using GitHub Actions runners, and produces pull requests as output. Interacting with the agent is simple: use comments on issues and PRs.

Connect Claude Code Action to SAP AI Core

Out of the box, Claude Code Action supports multiple cloud providers: Anthropic API, Amazon Bedrock, Google Vertex AI, and Microsoft Foundry. It, however, does not natively support SAP AI Core. To bridge this gap, we extended the action with a proxy that runs as a sidecar during the GitHub Action execution. It accepts requests in Anthropic format and forwards them to SAP AI Core via LiteLLM:


 

Architecture: Extending Claude Code Actions to Support SAP AI Core through a LiteLLM Proxy

 

From Claude Code's perspective, it is talking to the standard Anthropic API. The proxy handles SAP AI Core authentication and request routing transparently. Feel free to have a look at how we implemented the necessary changes here.

Using this solution, connecting SAP AI Core to Claude Code Action is as easy as setting `use_litellm` to `"true"` in the workflow configuration. You don't need to do any additional setup for the proxy itself.

 

Set Up the Workflow

Here is what our GitHub Actions workflow file (`.github/workflows/claude-code.yml`) in the consuming repository looks like:

 
Code Snippet: Setting Up a GitHub Actions Workflow to Enable Claude Code Actions Powered by SAP AI Core
name: Claude Code

on:
  issue_comment:
    types: [created]
  issues:
    types: [opened, assigned]
  pull_request_review_comment:
    types: [created]
  pull_request_review:
    types: [submitted]

jobs:
  claude:
    runs-on: [self-hosted, solinas]
    permissions:
      contents: write
      pull-requests: write
      issues: write
      id-token: write
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
        with:
          fetch-depth: 1

      - uses: <your-github-org>/claude-code-action@main
        with:
          trigger_phrase: "@claude"
          use_litellm: "true"
          litellm_model: "sap/anthropic--claude-4.6-sonnet"
          github_token: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
        env:
          AICORE_AUTH_URL: ${{ vars.AICORE_AUTH_URL }}
          AICORE_BASE_URL: ${{ vars.AICORE_BASE_URL }}
          AICORE_CLIENT_ID: ${{ vars.AICORE_CLIENT_ID }}
          AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET: ${{ secrets.AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET }}​
 

The `use_litellm` and `litellm_model` inputs activate the proxy and tell it which model to request from SAP AI Core. The `AICORE_*` environment variables handle authentication. Everything else is standard Claude Code Action configuration. The `on:` block defines which GitHub events trigger the agent, and `trigger_phrase` sets the keyword that activates it in comments.
 
Once committed, writing `@claude` in an issue or PR comment triggers the agent. For fully automated workflows, like reviewing every PR or triaging new issues, you can use agent mode by providing a `prompt` input instead of relying on mentions.
 

(Optional) Enable Session Continuity

By default, each `@claude` invocation starts a fresh session. By adding a cache step for Claude's session data and the `--continue` flag, follow-up mentions under the same issue or PR resume the previous conversation instead of starting over. This is particularly useful when iterating on a task across multiple comments. Two steps were necessary to enable our experimental setup to continue sessions. First, we added a post-checkout script:
 
 
Code Snippet: Post-Checkout Script for Claude Session Caching
- name: Cache Claude Session
  id: cache_session
  continue-on-error: true
  uses: actions/cache@v5
  with:
    path: ~/.claude/projects
    key: claude-${{ github.event.pull_request.number && 'pr' || 'issue' }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.event.issue.number }}-${{ github.run_id }}-${{ github.run_attempt }}
    restore-keys: |
            claude-${{ github.event.pull_request.number && 'pr' || 'issue' }}-${{ github.event.pull_request.number || github.event.issue.number }}-

 

Second, we added `claude_args` to the `with` section of the Claude Code Action itself:


Code Snippet: Adapt Claude Code Action for Claude Session Caching

claude_args: |
  --continue
 

Outlook


The broad spectrum of reports ranging from positive to rather discouraging effects of (autonomous) coding agents on development speed and quality, leave plenty of room for further investigation and optimization. This calls for your own stories and experiences! Whether you start with a setup like the one described above or explore alternative frameworks, now is the perfect time to gain first experiences, establish learnings and best practices, and pave the way for new ways of developing software securely and autonomously. We will definitely continue exploring this space and are curious to hear your thoughts and experiences in the comments.

Reach out and tell us what works best for you!

 
# CTO Research and Innovation Munich
@ladenaw 
@dDaniel 
@_Ph1pp 
@Martin_Obwexer

entry.title: SAP Community Enhancements: Improved Discovery and a Cleaner Experience
entry.content:
We are happy to introduce our latest Community enhancements, designed to streamline user engagement and modernize content discovery. By reducing friction for anonymous visitors with more accessible "Join" and "Subscribe" prompts, the platform now offers a smoother path to participation, complemented by a refined "Recent Activity" feed that prioritizes timely, high-engagement discussions from the last 15 days. Beyond these navigation improvements, users will find a more organized Events Calendar, improved read/unread visibility for blog posts, and a decluttered homepage interface that emphasizes essential announcements. Together, these UI refinements and consistency fixes ensure a more intuitive, stable, and focused environment for both new and long-standing community members.

Check out all the details now:
 
Easier Joining and Following (Even When Not Logged In)
New and anonymous visitors are now better guided to engage with the community:
  • Join Group buttons are now visible even when you are not logged in
    If you click “Join” while logged out, you’ll be prompted to log in or register, and the join action will complete automatically after login.
  • Subscribe buttons are now visible when logged out
    On tag pages, users can immediately see the option to follow a topic, encouraging discovery and return visits.
This makes it clearer how to get involved and reduces friction for new users.
 
Improved Content Discovery in Recent Activity Feeds
Recent Activity feeds across the main interaction styles have been refined to highlight more relevant and timely content:
  • Most viewed, most commented, and most liked filters now focus on recently created content (last 60 days), instead of pulling very old posts.
  • This ensures that highly engaged recent discussions are easier to find.
You’ll now see fresher, more meaningful content without changing filters manually.
 
A Cleaner, More Relevant Events Calendar
We’ve improved how events appear in the main Community Events Calendar:
  • Events created within Location Groups are now more selectively promoted to the main "All Location Events" community calendar
  • The main calendar focuses on broader, community‑relevant location events
  • All Group events remain fully visible inside their respective Groups
  • Existing event boards remain fully visible
Easier discovery of location events that are relevant to the wider community.
 
Better Read / Unread Visibility
To help you keep track of what’s new:
  • Read / unread indicators are now available for Blog Posts in tile view, extending the functionality already available in other feeds.
It’s now easier to spot new content you haven’t read yet.
 
Clearer Dates and More Consistent Formatting
Several refinements improve clarity and consistency:
  • “Last edited” dates now respect your personal date format preferences
    This applies especially to Knowledge Base (TKB) content.
  • Accepted solution indicators are now consistently aligned, even when titles span multiple lines.
Dates and visual cues should now feel more predictable and easier to scan.
 
Cleaner Homepage and Navigation Adjustments
To reduce clutter and improve focus:
  • The “users online” statistic has been removed from the homepage, simplifying the layout.
  • Content building blocks (CCB widgets) were repositioned to better highlight important announcements now that carousels have been removed.
Key information is easier to spot without competing distractions.
 
Polished UI Fixes and Small Improvements
A set of targeted refinements improves visual quality and usability:
  • Improved alignment and spacing in notification settings
  • Minor icon and layout adjustments for better consistency
  • Fixes to ensure interface elements behave the same way across pages
These changes don’t introduce new features, but make the experience feel more stable and refined.

entry.title: Re: External Billing API – credit memo equivalent of EOX1...
entry.content:
Good day @Kishoremalla 

Thank you for your question.

You should not reuse the invoice setup EOX1/EDX1/CIX1 with billing type G2. That is why the system raises VF 002. For an external credit memo without a preceding sales order, the supported standard route is to use BDRC to create an External Credit Billing Document Request. This should be billed individually into credit memo type G2.

As a next step, test the process with BDRC à G2 through the released billing document request process or through Manage Billing Document Requests. If BDRC is not available in you tenant, check which scope and communication scenario is activated in your system. If that still does not cover your requirement, you may consider raising a case or feature request.

2773986 - Problem with API in SD Billing Document Creation - Web service processing error - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me

 

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Managing Mixed Fiscal Year Variants on SAP Cloud ERP
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community,

We are currently designing an SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (SAP Cloud ERP) template for a group structure with 4 company codes. However, we face a challenge regarding the fiscal year setup.

We have two different fiscal calendar needs within the same tenant:

  • 2 company codes should use the standard calendar fiscal year
    (January 1 – December 31, e.g. K4)

  • 2 company codes should use an academic / non-calendar fiscal year
    (September 1 – August 31)

Current Limitation in CBC

As far as we understand, in Central Business Configuration (CBC), when defining company codes within the same organisational structure / country setup, the system does not allow assigning different fiscal year variants to the created company codes.

  1. In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition, what is the recommended best practice to manage company codes requiring different fiscal year variants in the same landscape?

  2. Is the intended approach to keep the leading ledger (0L) on calendar year and use an Alternative Fiscal Year Variant with a non-leading ledger for the academic-year company codes?

  3. If yes, what are the functional limitations of this design for:

    • Group Reporting / Consolidation

    • Asset Accounting

    • CO / Controlling periods

    • Project System / Budgeting

    • Year-end closing activities and special periods (13–16)

  4. If different fiscal year variants are fundamentally required at company code level, does this mean separate tenants / separate CBC projects would be necessary?

Objective

We would like to understand the clean-core / standard-supported architecture before finalizing the implementation design.

Any guidance from experts who managed mixed fiscal calendars in Public Cloud would be highly appreciated.

Thanks in advance.

SAP Central Business Configuration SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition

entry.title: Preliminary What’s New Information for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.3
entry.content:
Preliminary version of What’s New information and of the Product Assistance are now available for customers and partners to get a preview on the upcoming new and changed features of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.3.

You can find this preview on the current SAP Help Portal page SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition (see What’s New section “Preview of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.3”). To access the Preliminary What’s New information, please use the What’s New Viewer or the Release Assessment and Scope Dependency (RASD) tool.

The RASD tool provides you with a personalized and tailored view on What’s New information based on the activated and used scope within your system. The preliminary information for SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition 2602.3 will be available in RASD within the next few days. We recommend using RASD to prepare for upgrades and updates of your system and to focus on the new and changed features that are relevant for you. A valid S-User ID is needed to use the RASD tool and to view all the preliminary content stated above.

For any feedback you may have, try the Commenting Feature for What's New documentation in this release. See this blog for more information.

entry.title: Integration between Process Manager (BPMN modeling) and Process Intelligence
entry.content:
Hello SAP Signavio Community,

we are currently working on the integration between Process Manager (BPMN modeling) and Process Intelligence and would like to clarify some modeling prerequisites to ensure correct data extraction and interpretation.

Specifically, we are investigating the following aspects:

  1. Start and End Events in BPMN Models
    Is it a technical or recommended requirement that each BPMN diagram contains exactly one start event and one end event in order to be correctly interpreted or fully leveraged within Process Intelligence?
    Or are multiple start/end events supported without limitations in the context of data extraction and analysis?
  2. Scope of Model Extraction
    Which elements of a BPMN model are actually considered when being transferred or interpreted by Process Intelligence?
    • Is the extraction limited to the top-level (collapsed) process, or
    • Are sub-processes, lanes, and deeper hierarchical levels also evaluated?

  3. Best Practices for Modeling for Process Intelligence
    Are there any recommended modeling guidelines or constraints (e.g. regarding structure, granularity, or event usage) that should be followed to ensure optimal compatibility and analytical value in Process Intelligence?

Our goal is to establish a robust and scalable modeling standard that ensures consistency between modeling and analytics.

Any insights, best practices, or official guidance would be highly appreciated.

Thank you in advance!

Christine

entry.title: Re: custom field to the business volume selection criteri...
entry.content:
Dear @PASEK,

I verified the scenario in my Public Cloud tenant and the conclusions are as follows:

I created and checked a custom field using the business context Settlement Management: Condition Contract. The field is available at the condition contract header level and can be used in the header validation Badi WCB_CNDNCONTR_CHECK_HEADER. SAP documents this extensibility option for custom fields in condition contracts and the related app/API usage activation

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/7d9da5fff26b4461a9125401794c7be6/507e58626f49440486165c22b9cd5de8.html

I then verified whether the same custom field can be used as part of the Business Volume Selection Criteria in Manage Condition Contracts Sales Rebates. The field was not available there. The available criteria are controlled by the standard business volume selection concept, field combinations such as BusVolFieldCombnType and the process variant/selection logic

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/185067f43f564a46b421b76a680182f8/8cb830f6e6884e48924d1f37dfbbd4e1.html

I also checked whether enabling the available usages in the Custom Fields app makes the field visible in the Business Volume Base Badi. After publishing/enabling the field, it still did not appear in WCB_CNDNCONTR_CHECK_BVB_FINAL. This Badi exposes the business volume selection criteria table but not the custom condition contract header field. SAP documents the header check Badi and the business volume base check Badi as separate validation points during condition contract processing

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/1bb45c1149984b6c8f5ef955bd757756/e2bf412d9a9a42fe8ff3015474982468.html

Conclusion

There is no single released Badi interface where both datasets are available together:

  • WCB_CNDNCONTR_CHECK_HEADER exposes the custom header field (last one) but not the full Business Volume Selection Criteria table

 

  • WCB_CNDNCONTR_CHECK_BVB_FINAL exposes the Business Volume Selection Criteria, but not the custom header field

Because of this separation, a direct rule such as:

IF custom_header_field = 'Strategic'

   AND customer_in_business_volume_selection IS INITIAL.

cannot be implemented directly in one standard Public Cloud Badi

Recommended approach

Use WCB_CNDNCONTR_CHECK_HEADER for validations that depend on the custom header field

OR

Use WCB_CNDNCONTR_CHECK_BVB_FINAL for validations based only on standard Business Volume Selection Criteria fields available in that Badi, such as customer, field combination type, sign or other standard fields exposed in the BVB interface, for example:

    LOOP AT cndncontrabusvolselcriteriatab ASSIGNING FIELD-SYMBOL(<fs_busvolselection>).

      IF <fs_busvolselection>-busvolfieldcombntype = '0001'.

        fieldmessages = VALUE

        #( ( cndncontrbusvolfieldname = 'CndnContrBusVolSign'

             cndncontrbusvoluuid = <fs_busvolselection>-cndncontrbusvoluuid

             message

             = VALUE #(  systemmessagetype     = 'E'

            cndncontrmessagetext  = 'ERROR FOR SAP COMMUNITY' ) ) ).

      ENDIF.

    ENDLOOP.

 

IMPORTANT: A technical workaround that passes the value between the two Badis for example through global memory or a custom table just to connect both checks is not recommended for a clean-core Public Cloud implementation because the released Badi interfaces do not provide this combined contract

Summary:

The clean-core option is to separate the validations or map the business rule to a standard Business Volume Selection Criterion available in the BVB Badi

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Advanced FCV unrealized posting remains open after pa...
entry.content:
Hello @ElenaPuica,
Thank you very much for your detailed explanation. I now understand the processing logic much better, especially the separation between operational postings and valuation postings, and why the unrealized foreign currency adjustment remains until the next valuation run. However, while reviewing the configuration in parallel, I still have two questions .

Regarding the configuration step "Define Rules for Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation", specifically in the section "Assign G/L Accounts for Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation". In this configuration, four different G/L accounts can be maintained: Reset account for unrealized gain,  Reset account for unrealized loss,  Unrealized gain account and Unrealized loss account . My first question is related to the functional difference between these accounts.

My second question is related to account determination. Once these accounts are maintained in the Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation configuration, is it still necessary to maintain exchange rate difference accounts as well in the automatic account determination (transaction key KDF), or does the Advanced Foreign Currency Valuation configuration fully replace that requirement?

Any additional clarification would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you again for your support.

entry.title: Re: Customizing Notification Templates in SAP Cloud Identity Access Governance (IAG)
entry.content: @a_sudhir_kumar_patra In GRC we can have mail recipient filter setting in SCOT. Also, GRC AC allows us customization to on email alerts, but SAP IAG, being SAAS solution there are no such customization options available. Whereas you may check on the filter settings or permissions available on the email server side (SMTP/OAuth/MS Graph API) whichever you have used.

entry.title: Re: Total Value Not Maching on Sales Order
entry.content:
Hello!

In SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition:

  • The number of decimal places is controlled by the currency configuration
  • Most currencies (e.g., EUR, USD, GBP) are set to 2 decimal places
  • The system automatically rounds any additional decimals

This is a standard system behavior and cannot be changed.

Limitation in the Public Cloud environment:

  • It is not possible to increase currency precision from 2 to 3 decimal places
  • This restriction applies system-wide and cannot be customized

You can find details in OSS Note:

3632144 - Limitation of extending Decimal Places for Currency Amounts in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud

Link here:  https://me.sap.com/notes/3632144/E

 

Alternative approach: 

To handle this scenario, can be used a pricing unit (scaling approach) instead of changing decimal precision.

How It Works:

Instead of maintaining:

0.123 per 1 unit

You maintain the price as:

123 per 1000 units

(or similar scaling, e.g., 12.3 per 100 units)

 

  • The system still uses 2 decimal places, but the accuracy is preserved
  • It eliminates rounding errors
  • The total amount matches the customer PO

entry.title: SAP Ariba Sourcing: Missing suppliers during event invitation & standalone Sourcing queries
entry.content:
Atualmente, estou enfrentando um problema relacionado à visibilidade de fornecedores no SAP Ariba Sourcing e agradeceria sua ajuda.

1. Fornecedores ausentes da lista de convidados para o evento. Ao tentar convidar fornecedores para um evento de licitação (Prospecção), o sistema não exibe todos os fornecedores do nosso banco de dados. Temos um cadastro com mais de 2.000 fornecedores. Atualmente, em nosso ambiente de QAS, estou testando com dois fornecedores específicos. Ambos possuem um relacionamento comercial ativo na rede Ariba. No entanto, apenas um deles aparece nos resultados da busca quando tento enviar o convite. Qual configuração, filtro ou etapa de sincronização pode estar faltando para garantir que todos os fornecedores cadastrados estejam visíveis e disponíveis para convite?

2. Processo de licitação de fornecedores com o módulo Standalone Sourcing. Além disso, tenho uma dúvida conceitual: quando um cliente tem apenas o módulo SAP Ariba Sourcing implementado, como exatamente o fornecedor responde a uma licitação? Ele ainda usa uma conta padrão da Ariba Network ou o fluxo de trabalho é diferente?

Agradeço antecipadamente pela sua ajuda!

entry.title: WOCR Configuration to Create Separate Warehouse Orders per Customer in Same Activity Area
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I have a scenario in SAP EWM where I am picking the same product for two different customers. The key details are:

  • Two separate Outbound Delivery Orders (ODOs) are created for two different customers
  • Both ODOs are assigned to the same wave
  • Picking is happening in the same activity area
  • Automatic Pick Handling Unit (HU) creation is enabled

Requirement:
I need the system to create separate warehouse orders (WOs) for each customer, even though the product and activity area are the same.

Currently, since both deliveries are part of the same wave and activity area, they tend to get grouped together under the same WO via WOCR.

Expectation:
The WOCR should trigger in such a way that:

  • Each customer’s delivery results in a separate warehouse order
  • No mixing of warehouse tasks across customers

Question:
What configuration changes are required in WOCR (Warehouse Order Creation Rules) or related settings to achieve this behavior?
Should I use specific filters like:

  • Activity area + delivery/customer-specific parameters
  • Packing profile or HU-based grouping control
  • WOCR item filters (e.g., by document or partner)

Any guidance or best practice for handling this scenario would be helpful.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Re: yaai & yaai_ui – Open-Source AI Tools for ABAP Developers
entry.content:
Hello Christian:

Sorry to say it did not work. I now get a "File NOT found!" on the browser.
Here are the steps I took:

  1. Deleted the previous folder created with the original git clone
  2. cloned the https://github.com/christianjianelli/yaai_cockpit.git repository
  3. uploaded the webapp folder using report /UI5/UI5_REPOSITORY_LOAD
  4. Verified in SE80 that the webapp was in the YAAI_COCKPIT package
  5. Verified that the YAAI_COCKPIT services were active in SICF

Thanks again for your help

David

entry.title: BTP in Rio
entry.content:
🌴BTP in Rio | Tecnologia, Estratégia e Futuro em um só lugar

No dia 30 de maio, o Rio de Janeiro recebe um encontro essencial para quem quer ir além da operação e realmente entender o papel da tecnologia na transformação dos negócios.

O BTP in Rio chega com um convite direto: sair do básico e enxergar o SAP como plataforma de inovação.

🗓Data: 30/05/2026 
⏰Horário: 09h às 14h*
📍Local: A definir 
🎤Palestrante: Marcelo Fiorito

 

O que esperar:

  • Visão prática sobre SAP BTP no cenário atual
  • Como sair do “configurador” e atuar de forma estratégica
  • Clean Core, extensões e o futuro do ecossistema SAP
  • Conexões com profissionais que estão construindo o próximo nível da carreira SAP

 

Esse não é só mais um evento técnico.
É um movimento para quem quer se posicionar com relevância no mercado SAP.

Se você trabalha com SAP e ainda não está olhando para BTP…
👉você já está atrasado.

entry.title: Re: Different Table Partitionings in S4 HANA
entry.content: Multilevel Partitioning is generally recommended when a clear performance bottleneck is observed. This can occur when tables exceed 2 billion rows, or even at lower row counts with larger data size that impacts performance.

entry.title: Re: In Group Reporting, how can I restrict entering tradi...
entry.content: Since you are on S4 HANA 2020,  GRPL capability is not available in terms of derivation of partner consolidation unit. You could leverage validation / substitution group journal entry Fiori app to block certain combination of  FS Item and Partner Consolidation Unit, though it is a little bit passive approach.

entry.title: New Dates for Get Certified: SAP BTP Administrator Live Sessions
entry.content:
Since TechEd last year, the certification for SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) Administrator is available. It is a system-based assessment, where you complete tasks in an authentic SAP Software environment – a real SAP BTP global account.

Currently, I am delivering the second time the Get Certified: SAP BTP Administrator live session track, which introduces you to this new format of hands-on assessments and summarizes the topics covered in the certification.

Because we are seeing a high demand for these live sessions, we just added additional dates for you in July, September, and November in 2026. Please join me in the journey to become an SAP BTP Administrator.

💡Hint:
The core of the Administrating SAP Business Technology Platform learning journey leading to the certification is the Operating SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP200) course. Check-out my blog post summarizing BTP200.

entry.title: New Course Available: Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management
entry.content:
We’re pleased to announce the availability of a new course on SAP Learning:
Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management.

👉Access the course here:
Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management

This standalone course is designed to help professionals build strong, practical skills in configuring and managing SAP Customer Data Cloud for modern, compliant, and seamless customer identity experiences.

Why This Course?

Customer identity is at the core of trusted digital experiences. Organisations today must securely manage customer data, comply with evolving regulations, and support seamless interactions across channels and devices—all while enabling personalisation at scale.

Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management provides a comprehensive introduction to SAP Customer Data Cloud, focusing on real‑world configuration and administration tasks that directly support customer identity and access management (CIAM) scenarios.

Learning Outcome

By the end of this course, learners will be able to configure and administer SAP Customer Data Cloud to support secure and compliant customer identity management. Learners will design and manage account and data store schemas, including group models; configure site settings using the SAP Customer Data Console; and implement lite and full registration flows, authentication options, and identity journeys using UI Builder, while applying security and governance best practices.

Course Structure

The course is structured into logical units that guide learners from platform context through hands‑on configuration:

  1. SAP Customer Data Cloud in the Modern CX and Regulatory Landscape
    Understand how SAP Customer Data Cloud supports regulatory compliance and how it fits within the SAP Customer Experience portfolio.

  2. Console Site Configuration and Administration
    Learn how to navigate the SAP Customer Data Console, configure essential site settings, and work with customer data.

  3. Designing and Managing Data Schemas
    Design and manage account and data store schemas, edit schema fields, configure advanced data stores, and create group models for organizing customer accounts.

  4. Managing Customer Identity and Access
    Implement lite and full registration flows, manage identity journeys using UI Builder, and configure authentication and security options.

This structure ensures a clear progression from foundational concepts to practical identity and access management scenarios.

Who Is This Course For?

This intermediate‑level course is designed for professionals who work with SAP Customer Data Cloud and customer identity solutions, including:

  • SAP Customer Data Cloud administrators
  • Implementation consultants
  • CX professionals involved in customer identity, registration, and data management

Developer‑specific topics are addressed separately in other courses within the broader CIAM curriculum.

Part of a Growing CIAM Curriculum

This course is the first of four CIAM‑focused courses that will be added to the Learning Journey Implementing SAP Customer Data Cloud on learning.sap.com. Together, these courses will progressively expand the available self‑paced learning content and provide comprehensive coverage of SAP Customer Data Cloud capabilities.

Get Started

Whether you’re implementing SAP Customer Data Cloud for the first time or strengthening your existing expertise, Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management is the ideal place to start.

👉Enroll now:
Mastering SAP Customer Identity and Access Management

Stay tuned—more CIAM courses are coming soon.

entry.title: Re: Can reporting FS item REPT be used in the standard FS...
entry.content: booklet does not work for custom CDS query view in S/4 HANA 2023. As of S/4 HANA 2025, it is possible to create custom booklet which means it is possible to assign custom CDS query view to page.

entry.title: Re: Determine profit center and partner profit center on...
entry.content: It is possible with GRPL to have the derivation in place in ACDOCA. If GRPL is not in place, you need to reclass the data released from ACDOCA to ACDOCU with the prerequisite that the material field is available in ACDOCU.
